text:
Sivers Semiconductors (https://t.co/8VMNAF5r3U / SIVEF) has strong long-term growth potential in high-margin photonics and wireless tech sectors, but faces near-term dilution pressure, execution risks, and valuation challenges following this news.

What the News Means
•Bootstrap Europe is converting its full $12M convertible loan (part of a $17M refinancing from Feb 2026) into 22,847,044 new ordinary shares at SEK 4.77 per share.

•This eliminates that debt (positive for the balance sheet and interest costs) and simplifies the capital structure.

•Dilution impact: With ~296 million shares outstanding pre-conversion, this adds ~7-8% more shares. It’s already anticipated (disclosed in Feb 2026, with conversion price at a premium back then), but it increases the float and can create selling pressure if Bootstrap sells shares.

•The stock jumped ~15% today (aligning with your “16+ plus”), likely because the conversion removes debt overhang and signals confidence from the lender (who is now a shareholder).

Short-Term Outlook (Next 3-12 Months)
•Positive drivers: The conversion cleans up the balance sheet. The company has a massive ~$800M opportunity pipeline (up 77% YTD as of Q1), with catalysts like product ramps in 2027 (LiDAR, SATCOM, fixed wireless, defense, and potential AI/data center optics). Q2 results are due around early August.15
•Headwinds: Significant dilution (this event + recent/ongoing directed share issues for funding). The stock is volatile and has pulled back from highs near SEK 110. High valuation (very elevated P/S multiples relative to current revenue) makes it sensitive to any delays or misses. Recent sector selloffs in photonics (e.g., CPO concerns) have added pressure.11
•Expect continued volatility. Bootstrap may sell gradually (or hold), but the increased share count could cap upside until positive news dominates.
Medium-to-Long-Term Future (2027+)
•Bull case (optimistic): Strong execution on the pipeline could drive ~30%+ annual revenue growth, with breakeven/profitability targeted for 2027. Key markets (AI optics, LiDAR production ramps, SATCOM/defense) have high potential. Analysts forecast sharp earnings improvement. A Nasdaq dual-listing could boost visibility and liquidity.1965
•Base case: Revenue scales with product ramps, but competition and execution risks (delays in customer adoption) remain. Dilution from this and other raises will mute per-share gains.
•Risks: Still pre-profit with cash burn, dependency on converting the pipeline into actual revenue, sector hype cycles, and further equity raises. High valuation leaves little room for error. $Sivef

Overall: This news is neutral-to-slightly positive fundamentally (debt reduction) but adds immediate dilution. The company’s future hinges on 2027+ ramps materializing in photonics/wireless. It’s a high-risk, high-reward growth story suited for investors who believe in the long-term AI/semiconductor tailwinds and can tolerate volatility. Not financial advice—do your own research, consider the full financials, and monitor upcoming reports. $Sive

text:
https://t.co/0ncKgB2wBv

This is exactly the kind of experiment more people should be paying attention to.

Scientists used water waves to make an otherwise “invisible” quantum-style effect visible. A vortex in a tank acted as an analogue to the magnetic-flux structure in the Aharonov–Bohm effect. The waves did not need to pass through the vortex core for the global wave pattern to change.

That is the key point.

A hidden topological structure altered the phase of the wave environment, and the result became visible as system-wide nodal lines. Even more interesting: as the vortex strength increased, the number of rotating nodal lines became quantized.

To me, the deeper lesson is not “water proves quantum mechanics.”

It is that organized wave environments can reveal invisible structure through global diagnostic patterns.

Topology became visible.

Phase became visible.

A local organizing feature produced a non-local wave signature.

That matters far beyond this one experiment.

For ZPF Technologies, this is close to the language we are trying to formalize: dynamically organized electromagnetic environments should not only be analyzed by field strength or energy density. They should also be studied through phase, topology, boundary conditions, mode accessibility, noise structure, sidebands, linewidths, and residuals after standard explanations are subtracted.

If water waves can reveal hidden topological structure through nodal patterns, then advanced electromagnetic systems deserve the same kind of diagnostic imagination.

The frontier is not just building stronger fields.

It is learning how organized environments leave organized observables.

That is where topology, phase, stochastic dynamics, quantum vacuum diagnostics, photonics, plasma systems, and future fusion research may begin to converge.
#Quantum #quantumphysics #QuantumEngineering

text:
2026 PORTFOLIO UPDATE  Performance Metrics 

June 2026     :    6.39%
Harris YTD     : 45.79% 
S&P500 YTD: 12.84%  

My portfolio currently has 9 stocks: $nbis, $sofi, $mu, $aaoi, $asts, $oust, $pltr, $nflx, $onds

Currently 6/9 stocks in green, lagging only $sofi (who else!), $aaoi and $nflx.

Month Recap: It wasn't an easy month. It had a lot of ups and downs, and I'm really surprised that we closed the month in green. At one point I was -9%, but with the huge pump the last few days of the month, we managed to close in profit.

I will write down some thoughts of mine per each holding.
$nbis: This is the stock I have high conviction on. That doesn't mean I didn't trade it. I sold and bought quite a lot of times to make some profits and to initiate other positions. I've held it for one year now.
$sofi: I hold it from last year. Still in red, but I managed to bring my average down to $18.7, so I'm pretty close to breaking even. I love their CEO (@anthonynoto, send me a hat) as I believe he makes an extremely good job. The company is progressing really well, and the stock price will follow eventually.
$mu: I used to have SK Hynix, and I switched to have exposure to US markets. That's all. Same philosophy. Memory is in deep need.
$aaoi: A big player in photonics. All sector had a bad month. It will recover (I'm -14%), I'm not afraid.
$asts: I started buying when $spcx launched all the way to the bottom. I'm in green, I have a good average, and I believe a lot. Nothing more to say.
$oust: You see what happened last days with Physical AI and robots. Initially I bought in Aprin around $23 and sold at $36 for quick profit. Now I'm long here. Huge profit atm, I expect a pullback to take a breather.
$pltr & $nflx: I wanted exposure to both companies and I was looking for the opportunity. When $pltr dropped below 200week ema, I bought it and I have averaged. Netflix (last stock in red) gives me more opportunities for DCAing.
$onds: It was on my radar. I have issues with their CEO and his tactics (dilution and pumping), but at $7.9 it was a bargain and there was a huge disconnect between the company and the stock price. It's not a long-term position. When it reaches $10-$12, I planned to trim it.

Thanks for staying up to the end! If you enjoyed it, drop a 👋 emoji to see who read it.

Consider following me in order to be together in that journey. Today I reached 100k portfolio, so that's a huge milestone to celebrate!

$sofi, $mu, $aaoi, $asts, $oust, $pltr, $nflx, $onds Currently 6/9 stocks in green, lagging only $sofi (who else!), $aaoi and $nflx. Month Recap: It wasn't an easy month. It had a lot of ups and downs, and I'm really surprised that we closed the month in green. At one point I was -9%, but with the huge pump the last few days of the month, we managed to close in profit. I will write down some thoughts of mine per each holding. $nbis: This is the stock I have high conviction on. That doesn't mean I didn't trade it. I sold and bought quite a lot of times to make some profits and to initiate other positions. I've held it for one year now. $sofi: I hold it from last year. Still in red, but I managed to bring my average down to $18.7, so I'm pretty close to breaking even. I love their CEO (@anthonynoto, send me a hat) as I believe he makes an extremely good job. The company is progressing really well, and the stock price will follow eventually. $mu: I used to have SK Hynix, and I switched to have exposure to US markets. That's all. Same philosophy. Memory is in deep need. $aaoi: A big player in photonics. All sector had a bad month. It will recover (I'm -14%), I'm not afraid. $asts: I started buying when $spcx launched all the way to the bottom. I'm in green, I have a good average, and I believe a lot. Nothing more to say. $oust: You see what happened last days with Physical AI and robots. Initially I bought in Aprin around $23 and sold at $36 for quick profit. Now I'm long here. Huge profit atm, I expect a pullback to take a breather. $pltr & $nflx: I wanted exposure to both companies and I was looking for the opportunity. When $pltr dropped below 200week ema, I bought it and I have averaged. Netflix (last stock in red) gives me more opportunities for DCAing. $onds: It was on my radar. I have issues with their CEO and his tactics (dilution and pumping), but at $7.9 it was a bargain and there was a huge disconnect between the company and the stock price. It's not a long-term position. When it reaches $10-$12, I planned to trim it. Thanks for staying up to the end! If you enjoyed it, drop a 👋 emoji to see who read it. Consider following me in order to be together in that journey. text:
Half of the year is overwith. 

I have been fairly inactive over the last month as I spend time reflecting on the year, analyzing my holdings and seeing new opportunities for 2H. 

If I compartmentalize the year by months, I outperformed the majority in months 1-3 by having a defensive portfolio 25’ that was primary legacy manufacturers like $vrt $cat $gev. From April -> May I slightly underperformed, by not being able to pivot fast enough into emerging themes like memory and semis, only getting exposure to photonics through $aaoi. 

May -> present was OK, although my heavy positioning in $spcx ancillaries pulled performance down. Looking ahead I see no reason to 2H 26’ to be any different than what we have seen for the last few years. 

As I’m writing this I see Burry is now short my second largest holding caterpillar and I kindly ask him to accept my ballbag as I maintain bullish on the resurgence of heavy machinery and manufacturing names amidst an extremely pro America/infrastructure administration.

text:
Here's the post:

If I could only watch four layers in optical interconnect, ranked by bottleneck severity:
$COHR $LITE $FN $ANET

Nvidia just spent $6 billion locking down optical supply between March and June, and the market still thinks this is about transceivers.

The real story is InP substrates and laser chips — the stuff 20 layers upstream from what most people are tracking. Here's how I'd rank the supply chain by choke point plus earnings visibility over the next 12-24 months.

1. InP materials and epitaxy — the actual valve on compute scale. $COHR and $LITE aren't just CPO plays. They control high-purity indium phosphide epitaxy and high-speed laser IP that physically cannot be replaced in 800G/1.6T modules. Intel's CEO said it outright: InP is the non-negotiable material for solving chip-to-chip bandwidth. Nvidia locked each of them with separate $2 billion commitments and long-term offtake agreements. The Street prices them as module vendors. They're actually material oligopolists with compounding pricing power through every speed iteration.

2. CPO laser engines — the leverage on every GPU watt. $LITE and $COHR again, but now wearing their co-packaged optics hat. CPO moves the photonics inside the package, right next to the switch ASIC or GPU. That requires advanced packaging plus high-power lasers that don't cook themselves. Supply is sewn up. Goldman just put the scale-up interconnect TAM at $154 billion, and most of that growth is inside the rack and inside the super-pod, not between data centers. CPO sits in the middle of that build-out. Earnings inflection is 2026-2028 when hyperscalers move from pilots to volume. Risk is simple: if Nvidia capex wobbles, these names will gap hard because the trade is already crowded.

3. Module assembly and high-mix manufacturing — where cash flow actually prints. $FN is the TSMC of optical: low-drama contract manufacturing for every major module and CPO vendor. Margins are thinner than the chip guys, but revenue visibility is the cleanest in the whole chain because hyperscale deployment schedules are locked 18 months out. TrendForce just lifted 2025-2026 AI transceiver revenue from $16.5 billion to $26 billion, up 57% year-over-year. FN captures that with zero demand risk and no inventory. It's the only name here where you can model the next eight quarters with a straight face.

4. Switch and networking integration — the layer everyone already owns. $ANET is the consensus optical AI trade. Data center switching tied to optical back-end is a known quantity. Multiples reflect it. I'd rather own the three above because the market hasn't priced the material and assembly bottleneck the same way it's priced the switch attach rate.

The non-consensus bit: A-share money has been rotating from NAND and legacy semis into InP names for two quarters, but U.S. allocators still think optical is a 2025 story. It's a 2026-2028 margin story, and the earnings gap between upstream material plays and downstream module integrators is about to widen hard. I'm overweight the top two on that list until CPO production data forces multiple expansion.

Switch and networking integration — the layer everyone already owns. $ANET is the consensus optical AI trade. Data center switching tied to optical back-end is a known quantity. Multiples reflect it. I'd rather own the three above because the market hasn't priced the material and assembly bottleneck the same way it's priced the switch attach rate. The non-consensus bit: A-share money has been rotating from NAND and legacy semis into InP names for two quarters, but U.S. allocators still think optical is a 2025 story. It's a 2026-2028 margin story, and the earnings gap between upstream material plays and downstream module integrators is about to widen hard. I'm overweight the top two on that list until CPO production data forces multiple expansion."}}}, "rest_id": "2072048464947478850", "source": "Buffer", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "584", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
This 🎯roadmap🎯 for data-center interconnect speeds is also, in many ways, a roadmap for #siliconphotonics.  As data rates move higher, the industry will need more integrated optical solutions, including near-package optics and co-packaged optics. That is where #siliconphotonics becomes increasingly relevant.
I adapted this roadmap based on analysis from an investment bank, which states that it is built on company disclosures as well as its own research.
It is interesting to observe how the upgrade cycles are overlapping. According to this analysis 400G is already moving into decline, 800G is becoming the mainstream workhorse, 1.6T is entering mass-volume territory, and 3.2T is already visible before 1.6T has fully settled. Furthermore, 800G may remain mainstream for only around six quarters. That is shorter than the typical 2–4 year cadence often expected for a mainstream optical transceiver generation. This points to a compressed innovation cadence for the industry. Qualification, yield learning, supply-chain readiness, thermal design, packaging, DSP, and test infrastructure all need to mature faster than before. Only the 💪fittest can survive this race. My view is that integrated photonics is entering a sustained innovation cycle, driven by the bandwidth, power, density, and packaging pressures of AI data-center infrastructure. 
🚀 #siliconphotonics

As data rates move higher, the industry will need more integrated optical solutions, including near-package optics and co-packaged optics. That is where #siliconphotonics becomes increasingly relevant. I adapted this roadmap based on analysis from an investment bank, which states that it is built on company disclosures as well as its own research. It is interesting to observe how the upgrade cycles are overlapping. According to this analysis 400G is already moving into decline, 800G is becoming the mainstream workhorse, 1.6T is entering mass-volume territory, and 3.2T is already visible before 1.6T has fully settled. Furthermore, 800G may remain mainstream for only around six quarters. That is shorter than the typical 2–4 year cadence often expected for a mainstream optical transceiver generation. This points to a compressed innovation cadence for the industry. Qualification, yield learning, supply-chain readiness, thermal design, packaging, DSP, and test infrastructure all need to mature faster than before. Only the 💪fittest can survive this race. My view is that integrated photonics is entering a sustained innovation cycle, driven by the bandwidth, power, density, and packaging pressures of AI data-center infrastructure. 🚀 #siliconphotonics"}}}, "rest_id": "2072047497930047972", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "186", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Russia ITMO University: Optical Tractor Beam with Metasurfaces and United States: Boeing's Magnetic Tractor Beam Patent

#ufotwitter

It's fascinating milestone in the field of modern nanophotonics. Published in ACS Photonics in late 2018 by physicists from ITMO University, the research details how hyperbolic metasurfaces can be used to generate stable optical pulling forces—commonly referred to as an "optical tractor beam."

To understand how this functions, it helps to look at the mechanics of standard radiation pressure and contrast it with how these specialized, engineered surfaces flip that mechanic on its head.

The Problem with Normal Light

Under ordinary conditions, when a laser beam strikes a small dielectric particle, the photons transfer their linear momentum to the object. This creates a standard scattering or radiation pressure that pushes the particle away from the light source, along the direction of propagation.

Creating a "tractor beam" requires generating a negative optical force (a pulling force) that acts against the global momentum of the incoming light, drawing the particle backward toward the source.

How Hyperbolic Metasurfaces Change the Mechanics

A hyperbolic metasurface is an ultra-thin, artificially engineered material characterized by extreme anisotropy. This means its electromagnetic properties (specifically its permittivity, have different signs along different spatial axes—for example, acting like a metal  in one direction and like a dielectric in another.

When a small particle is placed close to this surface and illuminated by a standard unstructured light source (like a plane wave), the interaction relies on three core phenomena:

High Density of States & Asymmetric Scattering: The hyperbolic nature of the substrate opens up highly localized, high-density electromagnetic interaction channels (both surface plasmons and volumetric waves within the substrate). The presence of the particle scatterer near the interface selectively excites these asymmetrical near-field modes.

Momentum Conservation and Recoil: Because the substrate allows light to scatter preferentially into these highly confined, high-momentum modes directed forward along or into the surface, the law of conservation of linear momentum requires a balance. To compensate for the forward momentum of the tailored scattering channels, the particle experiences a net mechanical recoil in the opposite direction.

Broadband Pulling: Unlike older optical trapping configurations that require complex, actively modulated phase holograms or spatial light modulators (SLMs) to sculpt a specific beam geometry (like a solenoid or Bessel beam), this model utilizes the intrinsic dispersion of the substrate itself. This allows for stable optical pulling forces over a significantly broader spectral range using simpler incident light fields.

Why This Mattered

Prior to this approach, pulling particles with light required highly complex, bulky optical setups that restricted spatial movement and demanded high laser power, which could damage biological samples or nanoscale structures. By shifting the engineering challenge from the light source to the material environment (the metasurface), this framework opened up new avenues for nanoscale manipulation, chip-based sorting of cells or macromolecules, and nanoscale fluidic self-organization.

United States: Boeing's Magnetic Tractor Beam Patent

This is an interesting find. You’re looking at US Patent US6634603B2, titled "Magnetic dipole tractor beam control system", filed by The Boeing Company back in 2001 and granted in 2003.

While the phrase "tractor beam" instantly evokes sci-fi, the actual engineering framework here is grounded in classic electromagnetic field manipulation. Here is a breakdown of what this patent actually describes, how it works, and its intended application.

The Core Concept: How It Works

Unlike fictional tractor beams that use exotic energy to pull objects across massive distances, this system relies on the predictable physics of magnetic dipole interactions.

If you have two magnetic dipoles (which can be generated using electromagnets or superconducting loops), they exert a force and a torque on one another based on their relative orientation, distance, and field strength.

The patent describes a system designed to:

Attract or Repel: Manipulate the magnetic fields to pull a target object closer or push it away.

Control Orientation: Adjust the torque to precisely rotate, stabilize, or align the target object in three-dimensional space without physical contact.

Dynamic Feedback: Continuously measure the position and orientation of the target, feeding that data into a control processor to adjust the magnetic fields in real-time, preventing the target from drifting or snapping violently out of position.

The Intended Application

In space, docking two multi-ton vehicles (like a shuttle or capsule connecting to a space station) is incredibly risky. Mechanical docking mechanisms require physical impact, which can transmit dangerous kinetic energy, cause structural fatigue, or send both craft into an uncontrolled tumble if misaligned.

The "Tractor Beam" Solution:

By using this magnetic dipole control system, a chaser spacecraft could:

Approach a passive target satellite or station module.

Activate the controlled magnetic fields from a safe distance.

Gently capture, damp out any residual spin (tumble) of the target, and guide it into a precise mechanical lock without ever touching it until the final capture.

It's a highly sophisticated, non-contact magnetic tethering and dampening system—essentially a high-tech "soft-capture" mechanism for orbital logistics.

Tractor Beam Patent

#ufotwitter

It's fascinating milestone in the field of modern nanophotonics. Published in ACS Photonics in late 2018 by physicists from ITMO University, the research details how hyperbolic metasurfaces can be used to generate stable optical pulling forces—commonly referred to as an "optical tractor beam."

To understand how this functions, it helps to look at the mechanics of standard radiation pressure and contrast it with how these specialized, engineered surfaces flip that mechanic on its head.

The Problem with Normal Light

Under ordinary conditions, when a laser beam strikes a small dielectric particle, the photons transfer their linear momentum to the object. This creates a standard scattering or radiation pressure that pushes the particle away from the light source, along the direction of propagation.

Creating a "tractor beam" requires generating a negative optical force (a pulling force) that acts against the global momentum of the incoming light, drawing the particle backward toward the source.

How Hyperbolic Metasurfaces Change the Mechanics

A hyperbolic metasurface is an ultra-thin, artificially engineered material characterized by extreme anisotropy. This means its electromagnetic properties (specifically its permittivity, have different signs along different spatial axes—for example, acting like a metal  in one direction and like a dielectric in another.

When a small particle is placed close to this surface and illuminated by a standard unstructured light source (like a plane wave), the interaction relies on three core phenomena:

High Density of States & Asymmetric Scattering: The hyperbolic nature of the substrate opens up highly localized, high-density electromagnetic interaction channels (both surface plasmons and volumetric waves within the substrate). The presence of the particle scatterer near the interface selectively excites these asymmetrical near-field modes.

Momentum Conservation and Recoil: Because the substrate allows light to scatter preferentially into these highly confined, high-momentum modes directed forward along or into the surface, the law of conservation of linear momentum requires a balance. To compensate for the forward momentum of the tailored scattering channels, the particle experiences a net mechanical recoil in the opposite direction.

Broadband Pulling: Unlike older optical trapping configurations that require complex, actively modulated phase holograms or spatial light modulators (SLMs) to sculpt a specific beam geometry (like a solenoid or Bessel beam), this model utilizes the intrinsic dispersion of the substrate itself. This allows for stable optical pulling forces over a significantly broader spectral range using simpler incident light fields.

Why This Mattered

Prior to this approach, pulling particles with light required highly complex, bulky optical setups that restricted spatial movement and demanded high laser power, which could damage biological samples or nanoscale structures. By shifting the engineering challenge from the light source to the material environment (the metasurface), this framework opened up new avenues for nanoscale manipulation, chip-based sorting of cells or macromolecules, and nanoscale fluidic self-organization.

United States: Boeing's Magnetic Tractor Beam Patent

This is an interesting find. You’re looking at US Patent US6634603B2, titled "Magnetic dipole tractor beam control system", filed by The Boeing Company back in 2001 and granted in 2003.

While the phrase "tractor beam" instantly evokes sci-fi, the actual engineering framework here is grounded in classic electromagnetic field manipulation. Here is a breakdown of what this patent actually describes, how it works, and its intended application.

The Core Concept: How It Works

Unlike fictional tractor beams that use exotic energy to pull objects across massive distances, this system relies on the predictable physics of magnetic dipole interactions.

If you have two magnetic dipoles (which can be generated using electromagnets or superconducting loops), they exert a force and a torque on one another based on their relative orientation, distance, and field strength.

The patent describes a system designed to:

Attract or Repel: Manipulate the magnetic fields to pull a target object closer or push it away.

Control Orientation: Adjust the torque to precisely rotate, stabilize, or align the target object in three-dimensional space without physical contact.

Dynamic Feedback: Continuously measure the position and orientation of the target, feeding that data into a control processor to adjust the magnetic fields in real-time, preventing the target from drifting or snapping violently out of position.

The Intended Application

In space, docking two multi-ton vehicles (like a shuttle or capsule connecting to a space station) is incredibly risky. Mechanical docking mechanisms require physical impact, which can transmit dangerous kinetic energy, cause structural fatigue, or send both craft into an uncontrolled tumble if misaligned.

The "Tractor Beam" Solution:

By using this magnetic dipole control system, a chaser spacecraft could:

Approach a passive target satellite or station module.

Activate the controlled magnetic fields from a safe distance.

Gently capture, damp out any residual spin (tumble) of the target, and guide it into a precise mechanical lock without ever touching it until the final capture.

It's a highly sophisticated, non-contact magnetic tethering and dampening system—essentially a high-tech "soft-capture" mechanism for orbital logistics.

Herman “David” Froning: An aerospace engineer noted for publishing extensive theoretical work on field propulsion, vacuum engineering, and laser-driven space flight.

John Cole & Dr. Frank Mead: Both have histories connected to advanced or breakthrough propulsion concepts within institutional or military frameworks.

Alexander Karimov: Kepler Aerospace Advisor

Professor at MEPhI: Specifically within the Department of Electrophysical Installations, which deals directly with high-energy systems, particle accelerators, and plasma physics.

Leading Researcher at JIHT RAS: Since 1996, he has been a principal researcher at the Joint Institute for High Temperatures of the Russian Academy of Sciences (Division for Magneto-plasma aerodynamics and MHD energy conversion).

Laser, Beam, and Plasma Physics: Core disciplines involving high-energy directional beams and the manipulation of plasma states.

Hydrodynamic Coherent Structures: The text notes his specific work on the "Role of Initial Conditions in the Formation of Coherent Structures in Hydrodynamic Systems," an area of fluid mechanics critical to understanding how stable vortexes, plasmas, or fluid structures form and persist.

text:
$AXTI $COHR Do you believe this is last InP prepayment deal that will be cut over the next 5 years? We merely getting started.
———————-
BOTTOM LINE: This is incrementally bullish for COHR fundamentals but more strategically important than financially material. It confirms that 6-inch InP substrate supply remains a gating item for COHR’s AI optical ramp, validates AXT/Tongmei as a meaningful 6-inch InP substrate supplier, and shows COHR is using customer-style capacity prepayments upstream to lock supply through the next 3 years. It should be more stock-moving for AXTI than COHR, but for COHR it is a positive de-risking datapoint after a weak tape day, not a thesis-changing event by itself.

The agreement is straightforward but important. AXT-Tongmei entered a 3-year Master Development and Supply Agreement with Coherent, effective June 25, 2026, covering “mass development and supply” of 6-inch indium phosphide wafer substrates. AXT will expand capacity at its Beijing facility from 2026 through 2028, Coherent will make a $22.2885M prepayment for committed capacity, wafer prices are pre-established, and the prepayment will be applied against future product purchases. Coherent also gets refund rights if unused amounts remain at expiry or termination, and can terminate and recover unused prepayment if AXT fails to meet the capacity commitment for more than 6 successive months. AXT gets protection if Coherent misses minimum order quantities, in which case unused prepayment becomes nonrefundable and AXT can terminate. AXT must also offer additional capacity beyond the committed quantity to Coherent on the same terms. The full agreement will not be available until AXT files its Q2 2026 10-Q.  

The key interpretation is that this is not about outsourcing COHR’s differentiated InP device manufacturing. AXT is supplying 6-inch InP wafer substrates, the upstream crystalline substrate input. Coherent still does the value-added photonic device work: EMLs, CW lasers, photodiodes, PICs and related integration. That distinction matters because COHR’s moat remains process/yield/device manufacturing and vertical integration; AXT is enabling more wafer starts, not replacing COHR’s 6-inch InP fab advantage.

For COHR, the positive read is supply-chain de-risking. Management has repeatedly framed InP capacity as the key constraint in the AI optical cycle, while also saying 6-inch InP is central to the revenue and gross margin ramp. In the May earnings call, COHR said its 6-inch platform was producing EMLs, CW lasers and photodiodes, with yields exceeding 3-inch lines, first 6-inch-derived transceiver shipments already contributing to revenue and margin, and internal InP capacity expected to double ahead of plan and more than double again by the end of calendar 2027.   This AXT agreement strengthens confidence that substrate availability will not become the hidden bottleneck that prevents those internal wafer starts from scaling.

This also lines up with the J.P. Morgan investor-meeting takeaway that COHR had substrate supply “locked in” across 5 suppliers spanning multiple geographies, several capable of producing 6-inch substrates. The AXT filing effectively names 1 of those upstream supply arrangements and shows there is real money, fixed pricing, and committed capacity behind the company’s prior substrate comments.  

The less bullish read is that the deal confirms the bottleneck is real and persistent. Coherent would not be making take-or-pay-like commitments and prepaying a supplier for 3-year capacity if 6-inch InP substrates were abundant. Reuters recently reported that China’s InP export controls have disrupted the AI photonics supply chain, that AXT and Sumitomo dominate global InP substrate production, that AXT is a major supplier to Coherent, and that 6-inch InP wafer prices have reportedly risen sharply since China tightened export licensing.   The agreement helps COHR, but it does not eliminate geopolitical risk because the capacity expansion is at AXT’s Beijing facility, precisely the jurisdiction where export licensing and materials controls have become a live issue.

The deal is financially immaterial to COHR in isolation. COHR’s market cap is roughly $65B and the $22.3M prepayment is de minimis against the equity value, capex program, and cash balance. It is best viewed as a capacity reservation / working-capital deposit, likely recorded as a prepaid asset and then consumed through substrate purchases, not as an immediate P&L event. COHR was trading near $333 after a roughly -9.6% move on July 2, so the stock reaction is more likely to be driven by AI hardware sentiment, valuation, and semiconductor tape than by a $22M supplier prepayment.

For AXTI, however, the signal is materially stronger. AXT reported Q1 2026 revenue of $26.9M, so a $22.3M prepayment is roughly 83% of a single quarter’s reported revenue, even though it is not immediately recognized as revenue and the total contract value could be meaningfully different from the prepayment. AXT also said in April that it had completed a $632.5M capital raise to support Tongmei’s InP capacity expansion and 6-inch InP R&D, so the COHR contract provides named strategic-customer validation for that capital deployment.   AXTI was down roughly -12.9% on July 2, and this after-close filing could support a rebound when regular trading resumes.

The most important subtlety is that the $22.3M should not be treated as the total revenue opportunity. The filing says the prepayment is applied against purchases and ties to committed capacity; it does not disclose wafer volumes, per-wafer prices, shipment cadence, gross margin, or whether additional capacity is expected to be purchased beyond the initial commitment. Using Reuters’ cited $5,000 average 6-inch InP wafer price as a purely illustrative benchmark, the prepayment would equate to roughly 4,500 wafers, but that calculation should not be used as a volume estimate because actual contract pricing is confidential and the prepayment may only represent a portion of the broader commitment.  

The fixed-price element is meaningful. If spot 6-inch InP pricing continues to rise, Coherent has partially hedged substrate cost and capacity access. That matters for the gross margin bridge because COHR’s >42% non-GAAP gross margin path relies on 6-inch cost/yield benefits, higher 1.6T mix, internal sourcing, and better absorption. J.P. Morgan’s May conference note highlighted 42%+ gross margin tailwinds from the shift from 800G to higher-margin 1.6T, the transition from 3-inch to 6-inch wafers, and movement from external to internal sourcing.   The AXT agreement supports that framework, but the benefit likely shows up gradually through wafer starts, device output, module shipments, and margin conversion rather than immediately in the next quarter.

This is also a useful read-through to AI optical demand. Coherent is not just receiving long-term customer commitments; it is passing those commitments upstream. That is how a real supply-constrained hardware cycle behaves. Customers commit to COHR, COHR commits to AXT, AXT expands Beijing 6-inch capacity, and the entire chain becomes increasingly governed by deposits, capacity reservations, fixed prices, minimum orders, and termination protections. This reinforces the view that the AI optical cycle is capacity constrained rather than purely speculative order inflation, though it does not disprove some double-ordering risk.

The agreement also has a China-risk wrinkle that investors should not ignore. Coherent has emphasized U.S. manufacturing scale, Sherman 6-inch InP leadership, CHIPS-related support, and the NVIDIA strategic partnership. This AXT agreement reveals that even a U.S.-anchored 6-inch InP device strategy still depends on upstream substrate inputs from China. That is not inherently negative, because AXT is a scaled and strategically important substrate supplier, but it means export permits, U.S.-China policy, force majeure language, and qualification of non-China supply remain critical diligence items.

Relative implications are clear. For COHR, this is a positive data point for execution confidence and supply visibility, but not enough on its own to revise estimates. For AXTI, it is a much bigger validation event because it confirms that a leading AI optical platform company is willing to prepay for Tongmei 6-inch substrate capacity. For LITE, the read-through is mixed: positive for the industry because InP scarcity is validated, but potentially negative at the margin if COHR is locking a larger share of scarce AXT 6-inch output. For the broader optical ecosystem, the deal supports the view that the scarcity value sits not only in finished transceivers but also deep upstream in compound semiconductor substrates, lasers, isolators, PM fiber, ELS modules, and other enabling components.

Trading implication: this should help stabilize the COHR narrative after the July 2 selloff, but it is not large enough to single-handedly re-rate the stock. The better near-term setup is probably AXTI up more sharply on customer validation and COHR modestly supported, with the caveat that U.S. markets are closed Friday, July 3 for Independence Day observed, so regular-session price discovery is delayed.   COHR remains a high-conviction structural AI optical beneficiary, but the stock still needs gross margin progression, 6-inch output conversion, and FCF inflection to justify the valuation. This announcement improves confidence in the supply-chain bridge to that outcome.

Key diligence questions for Monday/next week: whether this AXT capacity was already included in COHR’s “5 substrate suppliers” commentary or is incremental; whether export licenses are already secured; what share of FY2027 6-inch wafer starts this covers; whether the agreement includes first-priority capacity rights or merely committed capacity; whether the fixed wafer prices are below current spot; how quickly AXT can qualify volume 6-inch wafers to COHR specifications; and whether any of the prepayment is ultimately customer-funded through COHR’s own strategic LTAs.

That matters for the gross margin bridge because COHR’s >42% non-GAAP gross margin path relies on 6-inch cost/yield benefits, higher 1.6T mix, internal sourcing, and better absorption. J.P. Morgan’s May conference note highlighted 42%+ gross margin tailwinds from the shift from 800G to higher-margin 1.6T, the transition from 3-inch to 6-inch wafers, and movement from external to internal sourcing. The AXT agreement supports that framework, but the benefit likely shows up gradually through wafer starts, device output, module shipments, and margin conversion rather than immediately in the next quarter. This is also a useful read-through to AI optical demand. Coherent is not just receiving long-term customer commitments; it is passing those commitments upstream. That is how a real supply-constrained hardware cycle behaves. Customers commit to COHR, COHR commits to AXT, AXT expands Beijing 6-inch capacity, and the entire chain becomes increasingly governed by deposits, capacity reservations, fixed prices, minimum orders, and termination protections. This reinforces the view that the AI optical cycle is capacity constrained rather than purely speculative order inflation, though it does not disprove some double-ordering risk. The agreement also has a China-risk wrinkle that investors should not ignore. Coherent has emphasized U.S. manufacturing scale, Sherman 6-inch InP leadership, CHIPS-related support, and the NVIDIA strategic partnership. This AXT agreement reveals that even a U.S.-anchored 6-inch InP device strategy still depends on upstream substrate inputs from China. That is not inherently negative, because AXT is a scaled and strategically important substrate supplier, but it means export permits, U.S.-China policy, force majeure language, and qualification of non-China supply remain critical diligence items. Relative implications are clear. For COHR, this is a positive data point for execution confidence and supply visibility, but not enough on its own to revise estimates. For AXTI, it is a much bigger validation event because it confirms that a leading AI optical platform company is willing to prepay for Tongmei 6-inch substrate capacity. For LITE, the read-through is mixed: positive for the industry because InP scarcity is validated, but potentially negative at the margin if COHR is locking a larger share of scarce AXT 6-inch output. For the broader optical ecosystem, the deal supports the view that the scarcity value sits not only in finished transceivers but also deep upstream in compound semiconductor substrates, lasers, isolators, PM fiber, ELS modules, and other enabling components. Trading implication: this should help stabilize the COHR narrative after the July 2 selloff, but it is not large enough to single-handedly re-rate the stock. The better near-term setup is probably AXTI up more sharply on customer validation and COHR modestly supported, with the caveat that U.S. markets are closed Friday, July 3 for Independence Day observed, so regular-session price discovery is delayed. COHR remains a high-conviction structural AI optical beneficiary, but the stock still needs gross margin progression, 6-inch output conversion, and FCF inflection to justify the valuation. This announcement improves confidence in the supply-chain bridge to that outcome. Key diligence questions for Monday/next week: whether this AXT capacity was already included in COHR’s “5 substrate suppliers” commentary or is incremental; whether export licenses are already secured; what share of FY2027 6-inch wafer starts this covers; whether the agreement includes first-priority capacity rights or merely committed capacity; whether the fixed wafer prices are below current spot; how quickly AXT can qualify volume 6-inch wafers to COHR specifications; and whether any of the prepayment is ultimately customer-funded through COHR’s own strategic LTAs."}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "core": {"user_results": {"result": {"__typename": "User", "affiliates_highlighted_label": {}, "avatar": {"image_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1996176688414367744/LXfA_lIx_normal.jpg"}, "core": {"created_at": "Wed Jul 02 10:44:15 +0000 2025", "name": "Serenity", "screen_name": "aleabitoreddit"}, "dm_permissions": {"can_dm": true}, "follow_request_sent": false, "has_graduated_access": true, "id": "VXNlcjoxOTQwMzYwODM3NTQ3NTY1MDU2", "is_blue_verified": true, "legacy": {"default_profile": true, "default_profile_image": false, "description": "Only on X, don’t trust fake accounts. text:
🔔 The Daily Semi-Cap — July 2, 2026

📊 SMH: $592.29 (-4.54%)

📈 Earnings:
• No new semiconductor earnings released today.

🔥 Major Developments:
• AXT-Tongmei signed a three-year 6-inch InP wafer substrate capacity agreement with Coherent, including a $22.3M prepayment tied to expanded Beijing capacity from 2026-2028. The optics supply chain is moving from spot-market scramble to locked capacity, which matters for Coherent, AXT, and the broader AI photonics bottleneck.
• Anthropic is exploring a custom AI chip with Samsung as a potential manufacturing partner, evaluating Samsung’s 2nm process and advanced packaging. If it moves forward, the read-through is bigger than one AI lab: frontier model companies are trying to control silicon roadmaps, and Samsung is still hunting for a marquee AI foundry win against TSMC.
• Nvidia is offering smaller GPU cloud providers financial backstops and revenue-share structures tied to unused GPU capacity. That supports near-term accelerator demand, but it also makes the AI infrastructure stack more circular: chip demand, cloud financing, and end-customer utilization are becoming increasingly intertwined.
• Meta said AI agent development over the last four months has not accelerated as expected, adding pressure to the capex ROI debate after reports that it may sell excess AI compute capacity. The takeaway is not that AI demand is dead; it is that hyperscaler capex now needs cleaner monetization proof, not just bigger buildout numbers.

text:
Hello darkness my old friend. 

Right when I think we’re done, you find a way to lure me back in!

I made peace with cutting $AXTI yesterday and it somehow finds a way to drag me back in. 

This deal is huge and the pre payment alone is nearly as much as their entire Q1 revenue. 

Not only that but it’s the catalyst it needs to shake off the recent fear around the AI infra trade and specifically photonics. 

This is my fifth time trying to trade AXTI in the past month. Is this time different?

text:
Fiber & Infrastructure Highlights – July 2, 2026

Conexon Connect Georgia completed its ninth FTTH project with Satilla REMC, surpassing 240,000 homes/businesses, 5,200 fiber miles, and delivering multi-gig to ~60,000 locations across 9 counties. Vocus announced ~A$500M ducted Sydney-Melbourne long-haul (6,912 cores, ready 2029, AI-driven). Cloud Capital/Realty Income formed a $6B data center JV (Virginia). South Korea outlined $919B mega projects with 18.4GW data centers by 2035. Nixxy/Tachyon announced a 1GW North Dakota campus. Tata advanced India-Singapore subsea.

Ongoing AI hyperscaler demand drives fiber supply ramps (Corning, STL, CommScope, Clearfield, Fujikura tripling production) amid persistent shortages (preforms tight through 2026+). Regional OSP/ISP activity includes Ziply Northwest, Ripple Fiber WA, Ezee Fiber, Brightspeed WiFi 7, Burlington Telecom 5 Gbps, Heartland Fiber Project, FiberLight West Texas, Big Fiber financing, Atomic Fiber Los Alamos, and various BEAD/FTTH builds. Manufacturing focuses on high-count, multi-core, photonics; FBA whitepaper on fiber-AI convergence and power/grid constraints add momentum.

 #FiberForge #FiberOptics #OSPISP

text:
【量子ハードウェア】
Pasqalがカナダで光集積回路（PIC）実装拠点を新設。790万ドル（約11.9億円、1ドル=150円換算）規模で量子・センシング向け製造基盤を強化。
（2026年7月3日 日本時間、Quantum Computing Report）

何が起きたか：
Pasqalは、買収したカナダ子会社Aeponyxを通じて、ケベック州ブロモンのC2MI（MiQro Innovation Collaborative Center）内に、光集積回路（PIC）パッケージングの専門拠点を設立すると発表した。 

このプロジェクトは総額790万ドル（約11.9億円、1ドル=150円換算）で、うち400万ドル（約6.0億円）は連邦・州政府が支援し、その中にはNGen（Next Generation Manufacturing Canada）からの300万ドル（約4.5億円）が含まれる。 

拠点では、シリコンナイトライド（SiN）フォトニクスを活用したPICの実装・組立ラインを構築し、レーザー制御モジュールなど量子コンピュータや量子センシングに必要な光部品の量産体制を整える。初期は試作・少量生産を担い、将来的には年間50万個超のモジュール生産能力を目指す計画だ。HOP Technologies、Phantom Photonicsも参画し、医療センシングやLiDARなど量子以外の用途も視野に入れる。 

読み解き：
今回のポイントは「量子コンピュータを作る」ことではなく、「量子コンピュータを量産できる部品産業を作る」ことにある。

量子コンピュータではチップそのもの以上に、レーザー、光導波路、ファイバー接続などの光学実装が製造ボトルネックになりやすい。特に中性原子方式は多数のレーザーを高精度で制御する必要があり、PICの高品質な実装技術は性能だけでなく生産性にも直結する。 

興味深いのは、この拠点が量子専用工場ではなく、防衛向けLiDARや医療センシング企業も巻き込んだ共通インフラとして設計されている点だ。同じフォトニクス製造設備を複数産業で共有できれば、量子産業単独では難しい設備投資の採算性を高められる。近年は各国が「量子主権」を意識したサプライチェーン整備を進めているが、この発表も製造能力そのものを国内に確保する流れの一例と言える。Pasqalにとっても、Aeponyx買収後の垂直統合を具体的な製造基盤へ発展させる動きとして位置付けられる。 

評価：
・業界インパクト：★★★
・注目度：★★★
・関連銘柄：PASQAL（未上場）

結論：
量子業界ではプロセッサ性能だけでなく、「安定して作れる製造基盤」が競争力になり始めている。今後は各社が光部品やパッケージング、製造拠点への投資をどこまで拡大するかが、商用展開の速度を左右する重要な指標になりそうだ。

出典：Quantum Computing Report
(https://t.co/kVpXEuzfHj)

"https://t.co/kVpXEuzfHj"}], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzI3OTYwNTcxMjIyNjMwNDA=", "text": "【量子ハードウェア】 Pasqalがカナダで光集積回路（PIC）実装拠点を新設。790万ドル（約11.9億円、1ドル=150円換算）規模で量子・センシング向け製造基盤を強化。 （2026年7月3日 日本時間、Quantum Computing Report） 何が起きたか： Pasqalは、買収したカナダ子会社Aeponyxを通じて、ケベック州ブロモンのC2MI（MiQro Innovation Collaborative Center）内に、光集積回路（PIC）パッケージングの専門拠点を設立すると発表した。 このプロジェクトは総額790万ドル（約11.9億円、1ドル=150円換算）で、うち400万ドル（約6.0億円）は連邦・州政府が支援し、その中にはNGen（Next Generation Manufacturing Canada）からの300万ドル（約4.5億円）が含まれる。 拠点では、シリコンナイトライド（SiN）フォトニクスを活用したPICの実装・組立ラインを構築し、レーザー制御モジュールなど量子コンピュータや量子センシングに必要な光部品の量産体制を整える。初期は試作・少量生産を担い、将来的には年間50万個超のモジュール生産能力を目指す計画だ。HOP Technologies、Phantom Photonicsも参画し、医療センシングやLiDARなど量子以外の用途も視野に入れる。 読み解き： 今回のポイントは「量子コンピュータを作る」ことではなく、「量子コンピュータを量産できる部品産業を作る」ことにある。 量子コンピュータではチップそのもの以上に、レーザー、光導波路、ファイバー接続などの光学実装が製造ボトルネックになりやすい。特に中性原子方式は多数のレーザーを高精度で制御する必要があり、PICの高品質な実装技術は性能だけでなく生産性にも直結する。 興味深いのは、この拠点が量子専用工場ではなく、防衛向けLiDARや医療センシング企業も巻き込んだ共通インフラとして設計されている点だ。同じフォトニクス製造設備を複数産業で共有できれば、量子産業単独では難しい設備投資の採算性を高められる。近年は各国が「量子主権」を意識したサプライチェーン整備を進めているが、この発表も製造能力そのものを国内に確保する流れの一例と言える。Pasqalにとっても、Aeponyx買収後の垂直統合を具体的な製造基盤へ発展させる動きとして位置付けられる。 評価： ・業界インパクト：★★★ ・注目度：★★★ ・関連銘柄：PASQAL（未上場） 結論： 量子業界ではプロセッサ性能だけでなく、「安定して作れる製造基盤」が競争力になり始めている。今後は各社が光部品やパッケージング、製造拠点への投資をどこまで拡大するかが、商用展開の速度を左右する重要な指標になりそうだ。 出典：Quantum Computing Report (https://t.co/kVpXEuzfHj)"}}}, "rest_id": "2072796057298424181", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "161", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
【研究開発】
米NSFが量子フォトニクス実用化へ400万ドル追加支援。GPS不要の量子ナビゲーションを視野に、最終段階では最大5,500万ドル規模の量子センター構想へ発展する可能性。
（2026年7月3日 〔日本時間〕・The Quantum Insider）

何が起きたか：
米ミシガン大学が主導する量子フォトニクス研究プロジェクト「QuPID（Quantum Photonic Integration and Deployment）」が、米国立科学財団（NSF）のNational Quantum Virtual Laboratory（NQVL）プログラムのフェーズ2に採択され、2年間で400万ドル（約6億円、1ドル=150円換算）の助成を受けることになった。これはフェーズ1の100万ドル（約1.5億円）に続く資金で、将来的には最大5,500万ドル（約82.5億円）のNSF量子センターへ発展する可能性がある。研究では、量子状態の光を利用した集積フォトニクスチップを開発し、GPSが使えない水中・宇宙・月面などでも利用できる高精度ナビゲーションや超高精度センシングを目指す。プロジェクトにはオハイオ州立大学、ハーバード大学、スタンフォード大学、パデュー大学のほか、Honeywell、TOPTICA Photonics、General Motors、Toyota、Raytheon、NASA Glenn Research Center、Air Force Research Laboratoryなど産学官が参加している。 

読み解き：
このニュースの本質は、「量子コンピュータ」ではなく「量子フォトニクス」の商用化に研究資金の重心が移っている点にある。今回の目標は演算性能ではなく、量子光学を使った実環境向けセンサーやナビゲーションであり、量子技術をインフラへ組み込む流れを象徴している。

特にNSFがフェーズ制で資金を拡大し、最終的には数千万ドル規模の量子センターを構想していることは、単なる大学研究ではなく、量産・実装を前提としたエコシステム形成を狙っていることを示す。さらに、自動車メーカー、防衛企業、NASAまで参加していることから、出口市場が明確な研究テーマとして位置付けられている点も重要だ。

量子業界では計算機だけでなく、量子センシングや量子通信が商用化を先行する可能性が高いと見られており、今回の支援はその流れを裏付ける動きといえる。 

評価：
・業界インパクト：★★★
・注目度：★★★
・関連銘柄：HON, GM, TM

結論：
量子技術の競争軸は計算能力だけではなく、センシングやナビゲーションなど現実世界の用途へ広がりつつある。今後は、このような研究プロジェクトが実証実験や政府調達へ発展するかどうかが、商用化を見極める重要なポイントになる。

出典：The Quantum Insider

text:
The Market That Changed Engines Mid‑Flight Strategic & Tactical Read of the Day 

Today's tape told a story that wasn't subtle: the market didn't break, it pivoted. Momentum the great engine of the 2025–H1 2026 rally finally lost altitude. Semiconductors, memory, photonics, and the AI‑power complex all slipped into a decisive unwind. META admitted AI progress has been slower than expected; photonics rejected their 21‑DMA; semis logged a second straight 5%+ selloff; MTUM printed its sixth rare >3% down day while RSP rose. That's not noise that's regime change.

Yet beneath the surface, the bull market kept breathing. Defensive sectors surged: healthcare, staples, utilities, materials. Equal‑weight advanced. The Dow hit a record. Investors didn't de‑risk they reallocated. This is classic bull‑market rotation: money staying in equities, but choosing different leaders.

Macro reinforced the shift. Payrolls softened, real earnings remain pressured, and factory orders ex‑transport were solid a mix that tempers rate‑hike fears without signaling recession. Micro confirmed it: hyperscalers stabilizing, domestic cyclicals strengthening, and median earnings tracking double‑digit growth. The bottleneck trade is crowded; the broader market is not.

Strategically, leadership is migrating from AI‑momentum to diversified, cash‑flow‑rich, domestically leveraged sectors. Tactically, expect digestion in semis and selective strength in defensives until Q2 earnings clarify whether AI re‑accelerates or hands the baton to the rest of the market.

Bull markets don't die when leaders tire they evolve when new ones emerge.

And to all American readers: Happy Independence Day may your portfolios enjoy some fireworks too. 🇺🇸✨

text:
Evercore ISI: Semiconductors

> The rise of Agentic AI in 2026 requires heavy orchestration, memory management, and tool scheduling. Al engineers project future CPU-to-GPU ratios will expand to 2:1 or even up to 10:1.

> The firm models the broader server CPU total addressable market expanding to an annual run-rate of 75 million to 110 million unit shipments.

> Evercore models a massive $275 billion Server CPU TAM by 2030. Top beneficiaries of this structural shift include Arm $ARM, AMD $AMD, and Intel $INTC.

> This quarter, 16 chip and component companies raised prices (including TXN $TXN up to +85% on legacy products, NXP $NXPI, IFX, and STM $STM).

> Lead times for certain analog/MCU components are stretching significantly to 30–99 weeks, with backlog building rapidly.

> The enterprise storage market is forecast to grow ~9% year-over-year in 2026, more than doubling the 4% growth rate seen in 2025.

> The total data center processor market is forecasted to reach $160–$200 billion by 2030.

> Custom ICs (ASICs) are expected to capture 35–40% of that total processor market, representing a ~$80 billion market opportunity by 2030.

> Driven by this AI buildout, data center compute revenue is tracking an 18% CAGR from 2020 to 2030, a notable acceleration from the historical 13% CAGR seen between 2005 and 2021.

WFE Forecast:

2026E: Base case is set at $140 billion (+28% YoY growth), with a bull case reaching $151 billion.

2027E: The base case is raised to $180 billion (another +28% growth step), with a bull case reaching $205 billion.

Memory: The absolute fastest-growing segment. Spending jumps from $40 billion in 2025 to $59 billion in 2026E (+50%), and skyrockets to $92 billion in 2027E (+55%), making up 51% of all WFE spend.

Leading Edge Logic: Grows from $38 billion in 2025 to $49 billion in 2026E (+30%) and $59 billion in 2027E (+21%).

Trailing Node: Consistently declines, falling from $33 billion in 2025 down to $32 billion (2026E) and $29 billion (2027E).

DRAM Exposure:

> ASML $ASML & Applied Materials $AMAT lead the coverage group in pure DRAM mix for 2025E, with ASML at 27% and Applied Materials (AMAT) seeing a significant expansion up to 25% (compared to just 16% in 2022).

> KLA $KLAC and Lam Research $LRCX trail slightly with 21% and 16% estimated 2025 DRAM exposure respectively.

Credo Technology $CRDO:

> Evercore models a massive 100% growth in AEC solutions for 2026, followed by an additional 60% expansion in 2027. Credo’s stickiness relies on selling complete system-level AEC assemblies rather than bare silicon.

> The firm expects Credo's optical DSP, silicon photonics, and ZFOptics modules to accelerate sharply, accounting for ~25% of total revenue by 2027 and scaling its absolute addressable TAM by 10x to 20x.

text:
I saw a few posts claiming $SIVE lasers are not good quality, or not comparable to competitors.

Maybe.

But before treating that as fact, I wanted to start with one simple question:

Who is leading the photonics technology?

As part of this series looking at the people around the table at $SIVE, I looked at Dr. Andrew McKee.

McKee is Managing Director Photonics and CTO, Photonics at Sivers.

He has been with the company since 2001 and was a founding member of CST Global, now Sivers Photonics.

Laser quality is not just one thing.

It is material quality, wavelength control, optical power, thermal behavior, reliability, yield, process repeatability, packaging, and customer qualification.

So when people debate Sivers’ laser quality, the relevant question is:

Do the people leading the photonics roadmap understand the actual physics and manufacturing problems behind III-V laser devices?

In McKee’s case, his background seems directly relevant.

Before Sivers / CST Global, McKee worked at $A, Agilent Technologies, in III-V optoelectronic device design and manufacture.

Plain English:

he worked on semiconductor devices that make, detect, or control light.

That is directly relevant because Sivers Photonics is not building normal silicon logic chips.

It is working in compound-semiconductor photonics, where materials like InP, InGaAsP, GaAs, and AlGaInAs are used because they are much better suited for making lasers.

And the word “manufacture” matters.

A laser company does not win by making one good lab sample.

It wins by repeatedly producing lasers with the right wavelength, power, efficiency, thermal behavior, reliability, lifetime, and yield.

So if the debate is about Sivers’ laser quality, McKee’s background is relevant because it sits directly in the world of III-V laser design and manufacturing.

His publication record is also useful because it translates directly into the “laser quality” debate.

One paper looked at the reliability of DFB lasers used in communications.

a laser is not good just because it turns on once.

For a customer, the real question is whether it keeps working over time, under heat, power, stress, and real operating conditions.

That paper dealt with device specification, failure analysis, and lifetime calculation.

That is basically the engineering version of asking:

How does the laser fail?
Why does it fail?
How long should it last?
Can it meet the reliability needs of the application?

That is directly relevant to Sivers because datacenter and optical-interconnect customers do not just need a bright laser.

They need a laser source that can be qualified, trusted, and manufactured repeatedly.

Another paper studied DFB lasers around 1310 nm.

1310 nm is one of the important wavelength regions used in optical communication.

A DFB laser is designed to emit light at a more controlled wavelength than a simpler laser.

The paper looked at how changing the internal grating structure of the laser changes the wavelength and performance.

That matters because in optical interconnects, wavelength is like a lane on a highway.

If the wavelength is unstable or poorly controlled, the signal can drift, interfere, or become harder to use.

So this connects to one of the key quality questions for Sivers:

Can the laser produce the right wavelength, with enough stability, across real operating conditions?

A more recent silicon-photonics paper looked at bonding InP DFB lasers onto silicon-photonic wafers.

silicon is great for routing and processing optical signals, but it is not naturally good at making light.

InP lasers are good at making light.

So one big challenge in silicon photonics is getting the InP laser and the silicon-photonics platform to work together.

That is not trivial.
The laser has to be placed very accurately.
The light has to couple efficiently into the waveguide.
The device has to manage heat.
The linewidth has to be controlled.
And the system has to be reliable.

That is directly relevant to Sivers because Sivers’ photonics story is about InP laser arrays and light sources used with silicon photonics and optical interconnects.

Overall, the person leading the photonics technology side at $SIVE does not look random.

He appears to have spent decades working on the actual problems that define laser quality.

I have next to no deep knowledge of the actual technology, and I may still be underestimating the complexity of it all.

But from the people side, Andrew McKee looks like exactly the kind of person you would want around the table if the question is whether Sivers Photonics has serious laser engineering capability.

This does not prove Sivers has solved every scaling, yield, or qualification problem.

But it does make me cautious about treating “bad laser quality” as fact without actual technical evidence.

In McKee’s case, his background seems directly relevant. Before Sivers / CST Global, McKee worked at $A, Agilent Technologies, in III-V optoelectronic device design and manufacture. Plain English: he worked on semiconductor devices that make, detect, or control light. That is directly relevant because Sivers Photonics is not building normal silicon logic chips. It is working in compound-semiconductor photonics, where materials like InP, InGaAsP, GaAs, and AlGaInAs are used because they are much better suited for making lasers. And the word “manufacture” matters. A laser company does not win by making one good lab sample. It wins by repeatedly producing lasers with the right wavelength, power, efficiency, thermal behavior, reliability, lifetime, and yield. So if the debate is about Sivers’ laser quality, McKee’s background is relevant because it sits directly in the world of III-V laser design and manufacturing. His publication record is also useful because it translates directly into the “laser quality” debate. One paper looked at the reliability of DFB lasers used in communications. a laser is not good just because it turns on once. For a customer, the real question is whether it keeps working over time, under heat, power, stress, and real operating conditions. That paper dealt with device specification, failure analysis, and lifetime calculation. That is basically the engineering version of asking: How does the laser fail? Why does it fail? How long should it last? Can it meet the reliability needs of the application? That is directly relevant to Sivers because datacenter and optical-interconnect customers do not just need a bright laser. They need a laser source that can be qualified, trusted, and manufactured repeatedly. Another paper studied DFB lasers around 1310 nm. 1310 nm is one of the important wavelength regions used in optical communication. A DFB laser is designed to emit light at a more controlled wavelength than a simpler laser. The paper looked at how changing the internal grating structure of the laser changes the wavelength and performance. That matters because in optical interconnects, wavelength is like a lane on a highway. If the wavelength is unstable or poorly controlled, the signal can drift, interfere, or become harder to use. So this connects to one of the key quality questions for Sivers: Can the laser produce the right wavelength, with enough stability, across real operating conditions? A more recent silicon-photonics paper looked at bonding InP DFB lasers onto silicon-photonic wafers. silicon is great for routing and processing optical signals, but it is not naturally good at making light. InP lasers are good at making light. So one big challenge in silicon photonics is getting the InP laser and the silicon-photonics platform to work together. That is not trivial. The laser has to be placed very accurately. The light has to couple efficiently into the waveguide. The device has to manage heat. The linewidth has to be controlled. And the system has to be reliable. That is directly relevant to Sivers because Sivers’ photonics story is about InP laser arrays and light sources used with silicon photonics and optical interconnects. Overall, the person leading the photonics technology side at $SIVE does not look random. He appears to have spent decades working on the actual problems that define laser quality. I have next to no deep knowledge of the actual technology, and I may still be underestimating the complexity of it all. But from the people side, Andrew McKee looks like exactly the kind of person you would want around the table if the question is whether Sivers Photonics has serious laser engineering capability. This does not prove Sivers has solved every scaling, yield, or qualification problem. But it does make me cautious about treating “bad laser quality” as fact without actual technical evidence."}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "core": {"user_results": {"result": {"__typename": "User", "affiliates_highlighted_label": {}, "avatar": {"image_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2047933364804395008/gbiwvuj6_normal.jpg"}, "core": {"created_at": "Thu Sep 03 16:38:42 +0000 2009", "name": "Askara", "screen_name": "softmaxedx"}, "dm_permissions": {"can_dm": false}, "follow_request_sent": false, "has_graduated_access": true, "id": "VXNlcjo3MTI5NzA1Nw==", "is_blue_verified": true, "legacy": {"default_profile": false, "default_profile_image": false, "description": "Ex-founder. Bastani answers: Do they understand real semiconductor execution? Karin Raj answers: Do they understand telecom, RF, and customer qualification? Heine answers: Can the financial story become cleaner? Helena answers: Can the board think strategically about M&A, capital markets, and which future deserves capital? The next one is Joakim Nideborn. And I think his question is slightly different: Can the $SIVE story move from pipeline and promise to numbers serious investors can underwrite? Joakim was added to the $SIVE board at the latest AGM. But the more important detail is that he was elected Vice Chairman. Sivers also said he would have primary board oversight for customers, employees, and investors in the Nordic region. That is specific. So I do not read this as just “new board member.” I read it as Sivers adding someone for the translation layer: technology story -> investor language pipeline -> revenue quality assets -> organization acquisitions -> integration growth plan -> cash flow That is Joakim’s lane. He is CFO of $HMS.ST. CFO since 2017. Deputy CEO since 2022. Before that, CFO at Beijer Electronics Group. Before that, management and strategy consulting at Axholmen and Celerant. So the pattern is pretty consistent: industrial technology, finance, operations, strategy, public-company communication. At $HMS.ST, Joakim has been inside a listed Swedish industrial technology company with real products, global customers, hardware/software, acquisitions, production, working capital, margins, and public-market reporting. From HMS’ 2025 annual report: Net sales: 2021: SEK 1.97B 2025: SEK 3.58B Adjusted EBIT: SEK 466M -> SEK 911M Operating cash flow: SEK 508M -> SEK 877M 2025 adjusted operating margin: ~25.5% So he has operated inside a company where industrial technology is translated into margins, cash flow, and public-market discipline. For $SIVE this is highly relevant. The second part is integration. HMS made its two largest acquisitions in 2024: Red Lion Controls in the US. PEAK-System in Germany. In 2025, HMS worked on integrating them into the organization. Red Lion and N-Tron became product brands under HMS. PEAK became part of the New Industries division. HMS also reduced its debt ratio from 3.4x EBITDA at the beginning of the year to 2.2x by year-end, helped by strong cash flow. That is not a small detail. Because $SIVE is also partly an integration story. MixComm brought US mmWave IP. Sivers Photonics / CST brought the Glasgow photonics fab. Wireless depends on manufacturing partners. Photonics needs capacity, qualification, and customer ramp discipline. So the question is not just whether the assets are good. The question is whether they become one coherent company. That is where Joakim’s background becomes interesting. Acquisitions are easy to announce. Integration is where the truth shows up. The third part is Nordic credibility. This may be the most overlooked part of his appointment. Sivers is talking about a potential US listing, PCAOB-related reporting work, and a more international investor base. But it cannot skip the Nordic trust layer. There is a running joke on X that Sweden is unusually good at doubting its own speculative small caps. And honestly, with $SIVE, you can see why that matters. The story has attracted global attention, but the home market still wants the boring proof: clean reporting, clear disclosure, capital discipline, governance, incentives, and whether the pipeline is being translated into numbers. That is why Joakim’s Nordic oversight role matters. Sivers specifically said he would have primary board oversight for customers, employees, and investors in the Nordic region. That is not a throwaway line. He has been CFO of a respected listed Swedish industrial-tech company. So he likely understands the language Nordic investors respond to: less hype, more numbers, more structure, more disclosure, more proof. If $SIVE wants to win trust at home while preparing for a broader international investor base, he is one of the keys."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2067530466337419464"}}, "rest_id": "2068979173910040972", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "24867", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2071713043990368488", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "14250", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
At @OpticaWorldwide  Quantum  Summit in Glasgow, one challenge surfaced repeatedly across discussions  on quantum computing, networking, and photonics: the industry needs  higher accuracy and lower loss.

As light moves from being a communication medium to becoming part of the computational fabric, the performance requirements for photonic circuits  are changing rapidly.

Quantum computing, quantum networking, AI interconnects, and optical processing all depend on increasingly complex optical paths. At that scale, even small imperfections can limit system performance.

This is why the next phase of the industry will not be defined by a single device. It will be defined by the manufacturing platforms capable of supporting the entire quantum and optical computing stack.

In his latest post, our CEO, Ofer Shapiro,  shares why the race among photonic foundries is becoming one of the most important competitions in the quantum industry—and how Quantum Pulse Ventures is approaching this challenge.

#quantum #opticalprocessing #quantumcomputing

According to Lightmatter CEO, Nick Harris, lasers are key and contribute nearly half of the potential revenue in all CPO technology

The industry's transition to CPO from near package optics will accelerate in the coming years, as copper is already reaching its physical limits

For a specialized company like $SIVE in lasers and photonics, this enormously elevates the strategic value of its technology

Every CEO from $LITE to $NVDA etc , is telling you that CPO is just around the corner and that it's going to be generational and inevitable

And you're shorting $AAOI and saying that $SIVE is going to 20 SEK hahahh

I'm not getting off the $SIVE and $AAOI boat

text:
Korea is putting US$1T+ behind #memory capex.

Everyone looks at the big equipment names: $AMAT $KLAC $LRCX.

Fair. BUT

The better question: who sits on the exact HBM chokepoint - less ran, cleaner, more elastic, and still checks the @aleabitoreddit playbook?

That’s why $FORM (#formfactor) is my #1 HBM pick-and-shovel name.

> Bottleneck strength: HIGH; every #HBM wafer needs to be probed before stacking; FORM sits at the test gate where yield risk gets caught early.

> Easy narrative: VERY HIGH; AI needs HBM -> HBM needs yield -> yield needs advanced probe cards. That is the whole pitch.

> Clean pure play: HIGH; advanced probe cards + wafer test exposure, without broad legacy semi-cap drag.

> Big 3 proximity: HIGH; direct exposure to #SKHynix / #Micron / #Samsung HBM ramps, with SK Hynix already naming FORM a “Best Partner.”

> Revenue speed: HIGH; FORM sits upstream of packaging; memory capex converts into orders earlier than many downstream HBM names.

> Growth runway: HIGH; Q1 FY26 already hit record revenue, while 2026–2028 rides HBM4, Texas capacity; estimate $1.6B revenue by 2030.

> Margin expansion: HIGH; gross margin is already near 49%; operating leverage supporting a path toward the 55% long-term target.

> MC elasticity: HIGH; ~$10B market cap; liquid enough for institutions, still small enough for real rerate. 

> Platform optionality: HIGH; HBM = first leg, #Foundry adds the second layer, and advanced packaging + #CPO/#photonics test can extend the story beyond the memory cycle. 

@jukan05 @ParadisLabs @ren_stocks @au_xbt @BULLOFBRITAIN

text:
July Picks: Already Firing

$MU + $DRAM : Closing green. The AI memory trade remains strong. My view: $DRAM has a path to $100+ by late July or August if the memory rally continues.

$AAOI : $132 → $150
$OUST : $42 → $54

$IREN : Still the laggard. EOY target: $120+. July could see a move into the $70–80 range if momentum builds.

$CRDO : I believe this is setting up for a move into the $300s. After the recent pullback from the low $300s, strong revenue growth, AI networking demand, and partnerships across the photonics ecosystem could drive another leg higher.

I am STILL adding more $CRDO & $IREN at these levels.

text:
Researchers in the Bozhi Tian Lab have engineered a wireless, bioinspired "artificial leaf" that harvests light to power next-generation biomedical devices. Published in Nature Photonics, the study by lead author Pengju Li and co-first authors Mengzhan Liufu, Cooper R. Johnston, Young-Woo Pyo, Yuze Zheng, Guangqing Yang introduces a breakthrough nanoplasmonic material—composed of microscopic gold and titanium dioxide structures—that efficiently converts light into high-performance electrical energy. The team successfully used this wireless bioelectronic platform in animal models to pace heartbeats and stimulate sciatic nerves via external light activation, offering a powerful new avenue for target-specific medical therapies like nerve pain management. Beyond biostimulation, the material can even serve as a secure human-computer interface, allowing users to input information via invisible light that is instantly decoded by AI. Read the full story at - https://t.co/Jm0RhAe5VG

text:
$SIVE

Lightmatter CEO Nick Harris: “Lasers are incredibly important. They represent literally half of the revenue opportunity of all of CPO.”

He also expects the industry to move from near-package optics toward CPO over the next several years as copper reaches its physical limits. For a pure-play laser and photonics company like Sivers, this dramatically expands the strategic importance of its technology.
@aleabitoreddit @StormDirac

text:
Buzzberg Daily Alpha.

We scanned feed across X, YouTube and Reddit, Substack .

The cleanest signal was rotation into “next liquid proxies” after AI semis: space, defense drones, humanoid robotics, Korea AI capex, and select compute infrastructure.

1. Space / defense kept attracting the highest-quality flow.

$RKLB  was the strongest space narrative. The thesis is no longer just launches. Speakers framed Rocket Lab as the only public, full-stack space pure play, with the Iridium/spectrum angle creating optionality beyond D2D: IoT, maritime, telemetry, PNT, drones and defense.

$ASTS  was tied to J-LEO subsidy speculation and broader space momentum. $ONDS was framed as a long-term drone/defense compounder, with near-term dilution/warrant risk but Russell inclusion and possible strategic buyer optionality. Drone baskets like $UMAC/$KTOS/$AVAV also came up as “future of warfare” watchlist names.

2. Humanoid robotics moved beyond hype into public-market vehicles.

$CCXI  / Agility Robotics became the main robotics discussion. Bulls like the first US-listed humanoid pure-play angle, Amazon/Nvidia backing, ~$300M backlog, H2 2026 production scaling and RaaS unit economics.

The caution: some speakers openly dislike the SPAC quality/valuation but still expect a speculative run because the public-market wrapper is scarce.

$BOT also came up as a liquid way to own private robotics markups over the next 12-18 months.

3. Korea AI capex was the most important YouTube/macro signal.

Bloomberg and Korean YouTube focused on South Korea’s massive AI infrastructure plan: Samsung/SK Hynix, chip fabs, data centers, renewables and power equipment. The read-through is broad: memory, equipment, power grid, shipbuilding, and domestic Korean suppliers.

Twitter translated that into $MU/$SNDK/DRAM upside overnight. Korean channels also highlighted power equipment and shipbuilders as beneficiaries of AI data-center power shortages and government-backed capex.

4. AI compute relative value is back.

$PENG had the most interesting under-the-radar thesis: possible Middle East sovereign AI breadcrumbs through SK Telecom, MWC 2026 meetings, and Blackwell cluster integration.

$CRWV vs $NBIS was framed as a relative-value trade: CRWV allegedly trades at a large discount to NBIS on 2030 deployed-capacity revenue assumptions, while leverage concerns should fade if revenue scales.

$MSFT also reappeared as a “capex misunderstood” long: Reddit and Twitter both argued the market is penalizing AI buildout the same way it misread Amazon’s reinvestment cycle.

5. Select semis are separating from the weak photonics tape.

Broad optics/photonics was still treated cautiously, but $MXL and $QCOM stood out.

$MXL: Panther/OpenZFS validation at Los Alamos was cited as real proof for the CPU/storage offload thesis.

$QCOM: speakers highlighted automotive design wins, data-center revenue ramp, and HBC/near-memory compute as a lower-power inference architecture.

6. Single-name alpha:

$ABVX was the cleanest biotech catalyst: speakers argued the cancer/NMSC safety concern did not replicate, the label is cleaner, and UC efficacy supports a rerate.

$FCEL stayed alive as a speculative carbon-capture setup tied to the Exxon Rotterdam pilot. One speaker sold tactically to rotate into robotics, but the core FCEL thesis was not described as broken.

$BB had a strong QNX/software thread: autos, robotics, industrial automation, Canada/Japan trade mission deals, and high-margin software model.

$SMCI was the clearest avoid: Taiwan raids, chip-smuggling probe, accounting history, auditor issues. The message was simple: governance risk overwhelms AI exposure.
