text:
#truefreefinance  (June 27.2026 )Robotics is quietly emerging as the next major wave in tech investing
Recent data from Andreessen Horowitz and PitchBook (March update) suggests that investment activity in robotics is accelerating, with both funding and deal flow continuing to rise.
What makes this trend particularly interesting is its connection to the broader AI infrastructure cycle.
Many of the same sectors benefiting from AI data center expansion are also positioned to play a role in robotics and humanoid systems:
• Memory (DRAM & NAND) → supporting on-device AI inference and storage
• Photonics & optics → enabling machine vision, LiDAR, and sensing systems
• AI compute infrastructure → powering autonomous decision-making in real-world environments
At the moment, most exposure is still indirect — through upstream suppliers or large tech companies investing heavily in robotics, such as Amazon and Tesla.
But the bigger question for investors is forward-looking:
If capital markets reopen strongly in 2026–2027, will we start seeing more pure-play robotics and humanoid companies coming to public markets?
If that happens, robotics could move from a niche theme to a full investment cycle of its own.
Not financial advice. Based on publicly available market data and industry trends.
#AI #Robotics #HumanoidRobots #Technology #Investing #FutureTech #StockMarket

text:
Nvidia quietly became the #1 data center Ethernet switch vendor — $2.1B revenue, 21.5% market share, up from 4% two years ago. IDC calls it 'one of the most significant vendor landscape shifts' in enterprise networking history. The networking business tripled to $15B.

The obvious read: Nvidia is diversifying beyond GPUs.

The more strategic read: Nvidia is identifying the next AI bottleneck before it fully materializes, then buying or building the solution before anyone else realizes it's a problem.

At million-GPU cluster scale, the binding constraint isn't compute or even memory anymore. It's the fabric connecting them. Copper interconnects hit hard walls on power, bandwidth density, and reach. GPU utilization tanks when all-reduce and collective communication across racks can't keep up.

Every cluster operator has experienced this: plenty of FLOPs, terrible effective throughput because the network is the bottleneck.

Nvidia saw this coming when it bought Mellanox for $6.9B in 2019. Spectrum-X now generates more revenue than all Ethernet networking peers combined, per Jensen. The company is vertically integrating end-to-end cluster fabric the same way it integrated CUDA into the software stack.

The next phase: photonics. Nvidia's CPO roadmap with TSMC COUPE is preparing for optical interconnects at the rack level. Bandwidth per AI rack goes from ~130 TB/s to multiples beyond that.

Here's the under-covered part: the photonics supply chain — lasers, silicon photonics foundries, fiber attach, packaging — is even earlier in its high-volume ramp than HBM was in 2024. The qualification cycles are longer, yields lower, and the ecosystem less mature.

The AI bottleneck narrative has shifted: compute → memory → networking → optics.

$15B in networking revenue is the market signaling the next constraint. The companies that solve the photonics supply chain will be the Micron and SK Hynix of the next cycle.

#Nvidia #Photonics #AIChips #Networking

text:
Retail is waiting for pure-play robotics IPOs, while institutional money is quietly locking up the raw physical supply chains today.

The vertical spike highlighted confirms a massive paradigm shift: capital is moving aggressively into physical, embodied AI. As the first pure-play humanoid exits open the liquidity floodgates for H2 into 2027, the structural allocation war we are seeing in data centers will expand exponentially into robotics.

The smart money is front-running this cross-exposure overlap through critical upstream hardware:
 The Memory Squeeze Phase II: A humanoid robot running dense, real-time localized inference models requires up to a 10x memory multiplier over regular edge hardware. The exact same HBM, DRAM, and NAND components currently supply-constrained through 2027 to feed hyperscalers are facing an entirely new, massive downstream demand sink.
 The Photonics Vision Choke Point: You cannot run autonomous, real-time spatial manipulation on a gutted hardware spec sheet. Next-gen FMCW LiDAR vision and sensor frameworks rely directly on high-power DFB laser chips. Companies like $AAOI or $LITE that control internal MOCVD crystal growth foundries aren't just building for transceivers—they are the literal gatekeepers of the physical optical sensing layer that every single humanoid platform requires to operate safely.

The Upstream Component Leverage: Retail is waiting for pure-play robotics IPOs to buy the hype, completely missing that the absolute pricing power sits with the upstream component layer right now. Whether the end-program is run by $TSLA , $AMZN or a venture-backed pure-play, they all must secure raw, physical capacity from the exact same tightly contracted component suppliers.

The cognition bottleneck has officially shifted to a physical execution bottleneck. When the factories are fully booked through mid-2027 just to satisfy data center demand, adding a multi-billion dollar humanoid hardware ramp into the equation means the choke points capture all the structural alpha.

Accumulating the physical plumbing before the global IPO season reprices the entire supply chain. Long the bottlenecks. 📈🤖🧠

text:
🧵 My personal market heuristic after tracking AI infrastructure themes closely:

Markets tend to price things in ~8 months in advance — except Space, Robotics, and Quantum, where sentiment runs well ahead of fundamentals.

Here's how I'm applying it across themes I follow: 👇

Photonics has a clear roadmap:

• 1.6T pluggables ( $LITE, $COHR, $AAOI) are hitting now
• CPO Scale-Out ramp: late H2 2026
• CPO Scale-Up (Ayar, Celestial, etc.): H2 2027

By the 8M rule, CPO Scale-Up names are still ~4 months early to position heavily.

Memory is mid-bottleneck and re-rating accordingly:

• HBM3e: now ($MU, Samsung, SK Hynix)
• HBM4 mass production: ramping now
• HBF: 2027

Same players across cycles = compounding opportunity as each architecture matures.

Glass Substrates:

• SKC Absolics early ramp: H2 2026
• Volume inflection: H2 2027–2028
• TSMC CoPoS, Samsung, others: 2027–2028

Machine/equipment suppliers here tend to see revenue before the volume ramp — worth noting for positioning.

Neoclouds: Q4 2026 earnings looks like a key inflection window for leading players.

In hindsight, March 2026 was the entry, not September 2025. The ~8M rule would've caught that.

Current repositioning focus:
• Memory
• Neoclouds
• Photonics
• Robotics

Preference for names capturing multiple themes simultaneously (e.g., pluggable + NPO + CPO exposure) and tactical exposure to bottleneck plays like advanced packaging and MLCCs.

Key personal lesson: Avoid heavy exposure to names 12M+ out. Early conviction is fine — overweighting too early is where sizing discipline matters most.

Still learning. These are just my own frameworks — not advice. 🙏

text:
Reflections in the Dip: Why My Conviction in $SIVE Has Never Been Stronger

I’ve been watching the recent pullback with a strange sense of calm.

Weak hands are panicking. That’s what they do when the price moves against them for a few weeks. But if you’ve been here before, you know this feeling isn’t new, it’s just the market doing what it always does to test conviction.

Over two years ago we were trading around 1.8 SEK. That drop felt brutal at the time. This one? This is noise compared to that. And yet the company is in a completely different place today.

We’re still in an industry that hasn’t really started yet. Photonics for AI data centers is still pre-ramp for most people. There are no shiny consumer products on shelves for the average investor to point at and say “that’s what I own.” No flashy earnings beats every quarter. Just deep technology, long qualification cycles, and a market that very few truly understand. Most people want to see something they can touch before they believe. That’s why these moves feel so violent, because the narrative isn’t obvious until it suddenly is.

That’s also why wireless has been so important. It’s been the quiet engine running in the background, delivering real revenue and building the foundation while photonics gets ready for its moment. Most of the market still doesn’t price that in properly. They’re too focused on the “when does photonics happen?” question and miss that the company is already executing on multiple fronts.

I expect photonics news and orders from Ayar Labs, $POET, O-Net, $AEVA and $JBL before the year is out. Those won’t just be headlines, they’ll be real validation that starts to de-risk the story and reduce some of this volatility. I’m 100% convinced of that. Not because I’m hoping. Because I’ve followed this company long enough to know how it works.

Nobody believes until it’s too late.

We saw it from 4 SEK to over 100 SEK. Almost nobody saw that coming when it was actually happening. The next leg won’t be any different.

There’s a lot of games being played right now, short sellers, institutions, traders all trying to take their piece. That’s normal when a stock has moved this much. It will stop when they’re done. Right now we’re also at a price level where a lot of serious money starts paying attention again. Don’t let the noise fool you.

Do your own work.

Read the presentations. Watch the CMDs. Look at the customer pipeline, the partnerships, the technology edge. Build your own picture instead of scrolling through what everyone else is saying.

If you don’t believe light is the future of high-speed connectivity, that’s fine. There are plenty of other sectors. But if you do understand the technology and what Sivers actually brings to the table, the differentiated InP platform, the asset-light model, the real design wins, then the choice becomes pretty obvious.

This is still a market that very few people truly grasp. A market that doesn’t even fully know how big it can get. And if you play it right, it rewards patience like few others.
I actually believe more in $SIVEF today than I did a couple of years ago.

Why? Because I can see the moves that are coming. Dual listing preparations are real. They’re not walking onto Nasdaq empty-handed. Watch what happens over the next few months. A lot of people are going to wish they had listened earlier.

For what it’s worth, I haven’t sold a single share. And I won’t. The day I sell is the day the company stops delivering. Not when the stock has a bad week. Not when the noise gets loud. Price and company are two different things.

This is still the early innings.

Stay calm. Do the work. Trust what you know.

text:
ちょうど昨日Zackの記事もありました、GFSとUMCの投資ロジックの違いについて

1. 成長ドライバー：シリコンフォトニクス vs. 22nm成熟プロセス
 GlobalFoundries (GFS) —— 高成長なニッチ市場への注力：
GFSはターゲットを絞ったアプローチをとっており、AIやデータセンターのインフラ需要を直接取り込んでいます。特に、光通信向けのシリコンフォトニクス (Silicon Photonics) 技術が牽引しており、2026年には同事業の売上が倍増、2028年末にはランレートで10億ドルを突破する見込みです。また、高周波通信向けのシリコンゲルマニウム (SiGe) ターンキーソリューションは2027年までキャパシティが埋まっています。AI光通信チェーンや欧米のローカルサプライチェーンのテーマに投資したい場合、GFSのストーリーは非常に魅力的です。
 UMC (聯電) —— 規模の経済とコスト効率：
UMCは成熟プロセスの雄であり、主力である22nmプラットフォームが業績を支えています。2026年末までに50社以上の顧客がテープアウト（設計完了）を予定しており、ディスプレイ駆動ICやマイクロコントローラ（MCU）など、幅広い汎用チップを網羅しています。GFSのような派手なAIインフラのテーマ性は薄いものの、Intelと共同開発している12nmプロセスが2027年に量産開始予定であり、将来的に高付加価値市場への参入ロードマップを描いています。

2. 財務パフォーマンスとバリュエーション
直近の2026年第1四半期（Q1）決算では、両社ともに売上総利益率（グロス・マージン）を約29%に維持していますが、その中身は異なります。
 GFS（バリュエーションは高め、長期的価格支配力）： スマートフォン市場の減速によりモバイル部門が逆風を受けているものの、デザインウィン（設計獲得）が前年同期比で50%急増しています。これは顧客の囲い込みが順調である証拠であり、長期的な価格支配力の強さを示しています。
 UMC（稼働率回復による短期的な利益爆発力）： Q1の設備稼働率が79%（前年同期から10ポイント上昇）まで回復したことで、営業レバレッジが効き、純利益が大幅に急増しました。また、UMCは伝統的に配当が安定しており配当利回りが高いため、インカムゲインを重視する投資家にとっても安心感があります。

3. どちらが今日の優れた投資機会か？
投資スタイルやポートフォリオの戦略によって結論が分かれます。
📥 GlobalFoundries (GFS) を選ぶべきケース：
長期的なアルファ（市場平均を超える超過リターン）を狙う場合です。
主力であるモバイル向けビジネスが2026年後半まで高一桁の減少を見込むなど短期的には逆風がありますが、米国・ドイツ・シンガポールに分散された製造拠点は、地政学的リスクを嫌う大手クライアント（Appleやルネサスなど）のニーズに合致しています。プレミアムなマルチプル（株価倍率）を許容し、AIインフラや光ネットワークへの構造転換に賭けたい投資家向けです。
📥 UMC (聯電) を選ぶべきケース：
景気サイクル好転による安心感と、確実なキャッシュフローを狙う場合です。
現在のUMCの主な懸念点は、シンガポール工場の拡張に伴う巨額の減価償却費であり、これが2026年後半の利益率を圧迫するリスクがあります。しかし、世界的な自動車、産業、家電向けチップの需要が本格的に回復すると予想する場合、稼働率上昇がそのまま利益に直結する高い財務レバレッジと、下値リスクを抑える高配当（防衛力）を兼ね備えた、バランスの良い循環株として非常に優秀です。

text:
Q1 2026 just printed a record: $16.3B invested across ~492 deals in robotics & physical AI. Humanoids are eating a massive slice of that capital. 

Deal sizes in the hundreds of millions are now normal. The steep upward trend you highlighted is accelerating into deployment phase, not just demos.
The cross-exposure angle is one of the highest-conviction parts of this story:

Memory (DRAM/HBM + NAND): Humanoids need serious onboard inference + storage. Same AI data center stack that’s already in hyper-growth directly feeds edge humanoid brains.

Photonics & sensing (DFB lasers for FMCW LiDAR): This is the vision/perception layer that turns clunky robots into dexterous, safe collaborators. Overlap with advanced auto/robotics supply chains is real and underappreciated.

Right now most investable exposure is still upstream components or inside large-cap winners like $TSLA and $AMZN (warehouse deployments scaling today).
But the pure-play IPO/exit wave is no longer theoretical:

Tesla is converting Fremont Model S/X lines — Optimus V3 production ramp starts Summer 2026. Public sales targeted toward late 2027. This is the strongest signal yet that 2026 is the bridge year from prototype to early volume.

Unitree (China’s volume leader — already shipping thousands of humanoids/quadrupeds with ~60% gross margins) filed its STAR Market IPO in March targeting ~$7B valuation. First major liquidity event.

Agility Robotics (Digit bots already working with Amazon, GXO, etc.) is going public via SPAC soon (expected ticker $AGLT).

Global IPO season for pure-play humanoids & robotics platforms is kicking off in H2 2026 and runs hot into 2027

text:
@OpenAI’s first custom chip, Jalapeño, built with @Broadcom for inference.

Everyone’s reading it as a shot at @nvidia. 

The deeper signal is that at this scale, the bottleneck isn’t just the compute, it’s the data moving between them.

Copper’s running out and Broadcom’s other big bet is the answer is photonics.

#photonics #copper #ai #openai

text:
Weekly portfolio update:

A rough week for the portfolio finishing
9.8% down 🔴

My photonics names $SIVE & $IQE took big hits this week: $SIVE down 29% & $IQE 7%.

Rolls Royce had a pretty flat week, down 0.14%🔴

All holdings in my pie finished red for the week too.

$VWRP -1.63%🔴
$SEMI -5.31%🔴
$QNTG -3.65%🔴

I crossed £2,000 last Friday, I’m now back under despite depositing £181 this week.

How was your week?👇

text:
🚨 A new AI infrastructure bottleneck just got its own ETF — backed by SemiAnalysis

Introducing $LAZR — the Tema Photonics & Optical ETF, launched in direct partnership with @SemiAnalysis, the leading independent research shop in semis & AI infra.

$LAZR is pure-play exposure to the optics layer moving data between chips, servers & data centers at AI scale — the second critical bottleneck after memory (its sister fund $DISK)

Top 10 holdings:

Lumentum ( $LITE) 14.6% — lasers/transceivers powering high-speed data center links

Cash  holdings 11.4%

Aixtron 10.5% — German MOCVD deposition equipment for compound semis

$AXTI AXT Inc 9.7% — substrates underpinning optical & power devices

$AAOI Applied Optoelectronics 5.8%

Furukawa Electric 5.2%

$TSEM Tower Semiconductor 4.1%

Landmark Optoelectronics ~3.9%

$AEHR Aehr Test Systems 3.5%

Yangtze Optical Fibre & Cable 3.4%

Why it matters?? 
📊Goldman projects the AI data-movement/photonics market to grow 9x between 2026-2028. 
📈As bandwidth needs outrun copper’s physics, silicon photonics stops being a niche play and becomes core infrastructure.

Statt auf den nächsten unprofitablen Hype zu hoffen, fokussiere ich mich auf diese 3 Kandidaten:
 $MSFT – bleibt das unzerstörbare Fundament der weltweiten Software-Infrastruktur.
$PLTR – beweist immer deutlicher, wie man mit KI-gestützter Daten-Analyse in der echten Wirtschaft massiv Geld verdient.
$SIVE – Sivers Semiconductors liefert die entscheidende Photonics-Lasertechnologie für KI-Rechenzentren. Die Volatilität nach der jüngsten Kapitalerhöhung bietet jetzt eine brutal spannende Einstiegschance.

Setzt ihr im 2. Halbjahr nur auf die bekannten Tech-Giganten oder habt ihr auch solche Small-Cap-Wetten im Depot?

text:
Daily Buzzberg Alpha — July 3

The tape sold AI/semis like Meta capex = overcapacity. The best sources read the same data as proof of scarcity.

1. The neocloud selloff looks like a misread. SemiAnalysis flipped the bear narrative: Meta isn't cutting AI capex — it's turning compute into a business. 5GW+ already contracted in H1, and 2027 capex could come in above expectations. Short-term compute reportedly trades ~4x long-term neocloud rates — that's not what surplus looks like. Read-through: $NBIS $CRWV $IREN $CIFR. The outlier: $ORCL, flagged for weak compute monetization vs peers.

2. Memory upgraded from "shortage" to pricing power. The HBM chatter got specific: 2027 contract talk now includes 2–2.5x price increases vs broker estimates. CNBC: the Apple AI iPhone cycle could lift memory demand ~50%, with Apple hunting supply even from Chinese makers. Note — the same Apple-China headline traded as a bear signal yesterday; today's read is demand outrunning supply. Macro counterpoint: pricing near peak, trade crowded. $MU  $SNDK , Samsung and SK Hynix in Seoul.

3. Korea is a market-structure trade now, not just a memory proxy. Local channels near-unanimous: the selloff was technical — 2x leveraged products, foreign flows, pension rebalancing — not broken fundamentals. Earnings + HBM LTAs + cheap valuation on one side, index-shock sensitivity to every AI rumor on the other. $EWY.

4. Photonics/packaging — best undercovered signal of the day. From SemiAnalysis' ECTC notes: Marvell custom HBM cuts host ASIC area ~60%, TSMC direct-to-silicon cooling handles >5kW, optical interconnects becoming the core scaling path. Supply-chain angle: $LITE demand reportedly 30%+ ahead of supply, $COHR locking wafer supply via $AXTI, $CIEN as the cleaner transport play. Also $MRVL $IQE $TSM $INTC.

5. Physical AI: quieter, higher quality. BlueCrest disclosed a 5.6% stake in $CCXI  (the Agility Robotics proxy). $ONDS expanded its counter-drone TAM from federal to state/local. $BOT as the public basket. $OUST cooling off on fresh mentions.

The non-consensus read: if compute is still scarce and Meta is monetizing it, yesterday's selloff was positioning and liquidity — not a fundamental break.

The HBM chatter got specific: 2027 contract talk now includes 2–2.5x price increases vs broker estimates. CNBC: the Apple AI iPhone cycle could lift memory demand ~50%, with Apple hunting supply even from Chinese makers. Note — the same Apple-China headline traded as a bear signal yesterday; today's read is demand outrunning supply. Macro counterpoint: pricing near peak, trade crowded. $MU $SNDK , Samsung and SK Hynix in Seoul. 3. Korea is a market-structure trade now, not just a memory proxy. Local channels near-unanimous: the selloff was technical — 2x leveraged products, foreign flows, pension rebalancing — not broken fundamentals. Earnings + HBM LTAs + cheap valuation on one side, index-shock sensitivity to every AI rumor on the other. $EWY. 4. Photonics/packaging — best undercovered signal of the day. From SemiAnalysis' ECTC notes: Marvell custom HBM cuts host ASIC area ~60%, TSMC direct-to-silicon cooling handles >5kW, optical interconnects becoming the core scaling path. Supply-chain angle: $LITE demand reportedly 30%+ ahead of supply, $COHR locking wafer supply via $AXTI, $CIEN as the cleaner transport play. Also $MRVL $IQE $TSM $INTC. 5. Physical AI: quieter, higher quality. BlueCrest disclosed a 5.6% stake in $CCXI (the Agility Robotics proxy). $ONDS expanded its counter-drone TAM from federal to state/local. $BOT as the public basket. $OUST cooling off on fresh mentions. The non-consensus read: if compute is still scarce and Meta is monetizing it, yesterday's selloff was positioning and liquidity — not a fundamental break."}}}, "rest_id": "2073137186501718220", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "470", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
WHILE EVERYONE PANICS OVER CHIP STOCKS, THE REAL AI INFRASTRUCTURE PLAY IS QUIETLY SELLING OFF TOO.

$AMKR Amkor Technology down 12.93%. Semiconductor packaging and testing, critical link between chip design and the end product.

$LITE Lumentum down 9.09%. Optical components and photonics for datacenters and telecom, key infrastructure for AI networking.

$VRT Vertiv down 3.5%. Power and cooling infrastructure for data centers, the backbone that keeps AI compute running.

$MPWR Monolithic Power Systems down 3.27%. Power management chips used across AI hardware.

Different layers of the same AI buildout story, all getting hit together.

DYOR.

text:
Posted by Antithesis Capital on LinkedIn in February 2026: 

Capitalizing on the Semiconductor Demand Cycle on the OTC market

SCI Engineered Materials (SCIA) is a global supplier and manufacturer of advanced materials for Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) thin film applications. SCIA is mainly focused on markets within the photonics industry. Their main product is Sputtering Targets which are specialized solid materials used in thin-film deposition processes called Sputtering for semiconductors, displays, optics, and coatings. 

How Sputtering Works:
- A sputtering target acts as the source material
- The target is bombarded with high-energy ions 
- These ions knock atoms or molecules loose from the target's surface
- The freed atoms travel through the vacuum and deposit onto a nearby surface (the substrate), forming an extremely thin, uniform film 

Demand for sputtering targets continues to increase as the Semiconductor boom accelerates. Sputtering targets are  essential for depositing thin-film layers (interconnects like copper, barriers like tantalum/tungsten, insulators) during wafer fabrication via physical vapor deposition.

SCIA trades on the OTC market with a market-cap of $26M. Meanwhile, their balance sheet consists of the following: 

Cash: $9.5M
A/R: $900K
Inventory: $3M 
Marketable Securities: $3.3M
Total Liabilities: $6.3M 

$4M of the $6.3M in Total Liabilities is Customer Deposits which can be better defined as Order Backlog and signals on-going demand for SCIA’s products. SCIA had $1.9M and $2.2M in Net Income in 2024 and 2023 respectively. They’ve made $1.2M in Net Income through 9/30/2025. 

SCIA’s closest NYSE/NASDAQ comp is Materion Corporation (MTRN). Materion is an integrated producer of high-performance advanced engineered materials used in a variety of electrical, thermal, and structural applications. 

MRTN trades at an above market multiple with a PE of 163. In addition, they were recently upgraded to Overweight by KeyBank. According to the report, data points suggest MTRN is well positioned to capitalize on a global semiconductor consumable demand cycle which could feature higher aerospace and defense budgets, expanding clean energy businesses, and opportunities for margin expansion. 

We agree with KeyBank’s thought process and think SCI Engineered Materials will have a chance to capitalize on this same demand cycle. In our opinion, SCIA is a much more attractive way to play this demand cycle thesis due their rock solid balance sheet and reasonable PE multiple (16x). 

We are not the only ones who think this. Management, on 11/17/25, announced a $1 million share repurchase authorization. Clearly, they see the same value. 

SCIA is set to report FY25 earnings this week.

"id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzMxMjAzODQwNTMwODQxNjA=", "text": "Posted by Antithesis Capital on LinkedIn in February 2026: Capitalizing on the Semiconductor Demand Cycle on the OTC market SCI Engineered Materials (SCIA) is a global supplier and manufacturer of advanced materials for Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) thin film applications. SCIA is mainly focused on markets within the photonics industry. Their main product is Sputtering Targets which are specialized solid materials used in thin-film deposition processes called Sputtering for semiconductors, displays, optics, and coatings. How Sputtering Works: - A sputtering target acts as the source material - The target is bombarded with high-energy ions - These ions knock atoms or molecules loose from the target's surface - The freed atoms travel through the vacuum and deposit onto a nearby surface (the substrate), forming an extremely thin, uniform film Demand for sputtering targets continues to increase as the Semiconductor boom accelerates. Sputtering targets are essential for depositing thin-film layers (interconnects like copper, barriers like tantalum/tungsten, insulators) during wafer fabrication via physical vapor deposition. SCIA trades on the OTC market with a market-cap of $26M. Meanwhile, their balance sheet consists of the following: Cash: $9.5M A/R: $900K Inventory: $3M Marketable Securities: $3.3M Total Liabilities: $6.3M $4M of the $6.3M in Total Liabilities is Customer Deposits which can be better defined as Order Backlog and signals on-going demand for SCIA’s products. SCIA had $1.9M and $2.2M in Net Income in 2024 and 2023 respectively. They’ve made $1.2M in Net Income through 9/30/2025. SCIA’s closest NYSE/NASDAQ comp is Materion Corporation (MTRN). Materion is an integrated producer of high-performance advanced engineered materials used in a variety of electrical, thermal, and structural applications. MRTN trades at an above market multiple with a PE of 163. In addition, they were recently upgraded to Overweight by KeyBank. According to the report, data points suggest MTRN is well positioned to capitalize on a global semiconductor consumable demand cycle which could feature higher aerospace and defense budgets, expanding clean energy businesses, and opportunities for margin expansion. We agree with KeyBank’s thought process and think SCI Engineered Materials will have a chance to capitalize on this same demand cycle. In our opinion, SCIA is a much more attractive way to play this demand cycle thesis due their rock solid balance sheet and reasonable PE multiple (16x). We are not the only ones who think this. Management, on 11/17/25, announced a $1 million share repurchase authorization. Clearly, they see the same value. SCIA is set to report FY25 earnings this week."}}}, "rest_id": "2073120384283807751", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "23", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The firm whose bearish CPO paper helped tank optical stocks just launched a photonics ETF and it's holding $SIVE.

Walk through what actually happened:

→ SemiAnalysis published a scathing piece on CPO delays and optical valuations. Optical names sold off 40-60%.

→ On June 30, SemiAnalysis co-launched the Tema Photonics & Optical ETF (NYSE: LAZR) — the first institutionally managed photonics ETF — in partnership with Tema ETFs.

→ As of July 1, that ETF holds 26,688 shares of $SIVE, bought around 48 SEK.

So the same research shop that helped drive the sector down is now buying into it through an institutional product — a 24-name concentrated fund built specifically for photonics and optical exposure.

I'm not going to call that anything it isn't. What I'll say is this:

When you get to publish the bearish note AND launch the fund that buys the dip, that's the most enviable seat in the market. And the name they put in the fund — out of every photonics company on Earth, in a concentrated 24-holding portfolio — was $SIVE.

An actively managed, SemiAnalysis-backed, institutional photonics fund looked at the entire optical universe and decided Sivers belonged in the 24.

That's not retail hype. That's a research-driven institutional desk putting $SIVE in the same basket as Lumentum, Coherent, and AXT.

The company keeps ending up exactly where the smartest money in the sector is looking.

$SIVE $LAZR

text:
Here's the takeaway that actually matters: $AAOI, $LITE, $COHR, $CIEN, $NOK are down 25-55% on sector weakness.

The photonics demand curve extends into 2027-2028 regardless of when CPO hits mass production. As clusters scale, copper hits a wall. Optical isn't optional. I'm buying the fear, not the narrative.

How far off their highs, and what you make just reclaiming them:

• $SIVEF $5.24 | down 53% | +115% to reclaim $11.25
• $AAOI $121 | down 48% | +93% to reclaim $234
• $CIEN $422 | down 34% | +51% to reclaim $638
• $LITE $725 | down 33% | +50% to reclaim $1,086
• $NOK $12 | down 31% | +45% to reclaim $17.45 (52w)
• $COHR $333 | down 24% | +32% to reclaim $440

And that's just to tag old highs.

text:
AXT เซ็นดีล 3 ปีกับ Coherent ขยายกำลังผลิตวัสดุชิปโฟโตนิกส์

AXT $AXTI เปิดเผยผ่านเอกสารยื่นต่อ SEC ว่าบริษัทในเครือ AXT-Tongmei ได้ทำข้อตกลงระยะเวลา 3 ปีร่วมกับ Coherent $COHR

ดีลนี้เกี่ยวข้องกับการพัฒนาและจัดส่ง substrate อินเดียมฟอสไฟด์ขนาด 6 นิ้ว ซึ่งเป็นวัสดุสำคัญในอุตสาหกรรม photonics หรือเทคโนโลยีการส่งข้อมูลด้วยแสง

ขยายกำลังผลิตในจีนเพื่อรองรับคำสั่งซื้อ
ภายใต้ข้อตกลงนี้ AXT จะขยายกำลังการผลิตที่โรงงานในกรุงปักกิ่ง ระหว่างปี 2026 ถึง 2028
โดยผลผลิตทั้งหมดจะถูกจัดสรรให้กับ Coherent ตามสัญญาที่กำหนดไว้

Coherent วางเงินล่วงหน้ากว่า 22 ล้านดอลลาร์
Coherent จะจ่ายเงินล่วงหน้าประมาณ 22.29 ล้านดอลลาร์สหรัฐ
เงินจำนวนนี้จะถูกนำไปใช้เป็นเครดิตสำหรับการซื้อสินค้าล็อตในอนาคต

อย่างไรก็ตาม เอกสารระบุเงื่อนไขสำคัญว่า
หาก Coherent ใช้คำสั่งซื้อตามขั้นต่ำไม่ครบ เงินล่วงหน้าอาจ “ไม่สามารถขอคืนได้”
แต่หาก Coherent เลือกยกเลิกดีลก่อนโดยไม่ผิดเงื่อนไข จะสามารถขอคืนเงินได้

ในทางกลับกัน
ถ้า AXT ส่งมอบกำลังผลิตไม่ได้ตามที่ตกลงนานเกิน 6 เดือน
Coherent มีสิทธิ์ยกเลิกสัญญาและขอคืนเงินที่ยังไม่ได้ใช้

text:
$IONQ is looking to become vertically integrated.

The acquisition of $SKYT seems like the missing piece.

It’s not.

$IONQ buys laser systems from Coherent and NKT Photonics.

Coherent is a US company that supplies lasers to $IONQ called Coherent Paladin.

The ultraviolet lasers produce light at specific wavelengths for the atomic transitions in barium and ytterbium that $IONQ uses.

Those are standard benchtop setups for general qubit manipulation. They’re massive and require lab environment to operate.

Then there’s NKT Photonics from Denmark. It supplies tailor made crystal fiber lasers with low noise. The goal here is to use the lasers in datacenters.

The setup is smaller so that the lasers can be mounted in a 19-inch rack with the other components of the quantum computer.

The lasers are ultra-stable and narrow-linewidth. It’s challenging to simply swap them with anyone else’s lasers.

Which makes $IONQ vulnerable to this specific supply chain.

Coherent relies on gallium, germanium, and rare-earth elements, most of which are mined in China. China suspended its total ban on exporting critical dual-use metals to the US until November 27, 2026.

What will happen after? Nobody knows.

We know from Coherent’s 2023 update that it had ample inventories and suppliers outside of China.

NKT Photonics sources nonlinear crystals used to build frequency converters from Europe, Japan, and the US.

The dangers here are regulations and delays.

NKT Photonics is a subsidiary of Hamamatsu Photonics. It has preferential access to Hamamatsu’s supply chain of laser diodes and optical components.

Trade wars between Europe and Japan are unlikely, but if they happen, NKT Photonics will be affected.

Finally, its lasers are dual use: They can be used in quantum computers and military guidance or radar systems.

Any sudden restrictions by Danish authorities could result in bottlenecks.

$IONQ is managing its supply chain nicely but dangers remain.

text:
Thread

Everyone keeps asking the same question.

"Which company wins AI?" 
$NVDA $AVGO $AMD $TSM $MU $MSFT $GOOGL $AMZN $META $ORCL $PLTR ?

I think that's the wrong question.

The better question is:

Which company becomes impossible to replace?

The AI infrastructure buildout isn't being constrained by GPUs anymore.

It's becoming constrained by everything around them.

Power.

Cooling.

Memory.

Networking.

And increasingly...

Light.

Most investors still think optical networking is about transceivers.

It isn't.

The real value is quietly moving upstream.

Every new optical architecture—whether it's pluggables, Scale-Out CPO, Scale-Up CPO, Near-Packaged Optics, or External Laser Source (ELS)—still depends on one thing:

A stable, high-volume CW laser.

Different architectures.

Same dependency.

That's where supply-chain mapping becomes interesting.

This is why I continue spending far more time studying suppliers than hyperscalers.

Hyperscalers change vendors.

Platforms evolve.

Chip architectures change.

But infrastructure bottlenecks?

Those usually last much longer than people expect.

The companies solving those bottlenecks often capture the highest operating leverage of the entire cycle.

This is exactly why I find $SIVE so interesting.

Most people still describe Sivers as "a laser company."

I think that's missing the point.

The product isn't the thesis.

Its position in the supply chain is.

If multiple AI networking architectures all require the same laser technology, then the value isn't coming from any single customer.

It's coming from becoming embedded across the ecosystem.

That's a completely different investment framework.

People also underestimate how infrastructure cycles actually work.

Revenue is always the last thing to arrive.

First comes R&D.

Then qualification.

Then design wins.

Then ecosystem validation.

Only after all of that do production ramps begin.

Investors waiting for obvious revenue growth are usually arriving after the market has already repriced the opportunity.

One thing I think the market still misunderstands is timing.

Many investors expect AI photonics to become a meaningful revenue driver this quarter.

I don't.

The real story is what happens when next-generation optical deployments begin scaling throughout 2027.

That's when supply-chain positioning starts to matter far more than quarterly headlines.

I've said this before:

I don't invest by asking,

"Who sells the most AI chips?"

I ask,

"Who does everyone else quietly depend on?"

Those companies rarely make headlines.

"Which company wins AI?" $NVDA $AVGO $AMD $TSM $MU $MSFT $GOOGL $AMZN $META $ORCL $PLTR ? I think that's the wrong question. The better question is: Which company becomes impossible to replace? The AI infrastructure buildout isn't being constrained by GPUs anymore. It's becoming constrained by everything around them. Power. Cooling. Memory. Networking. And increasingly... Light. Most investors still think optical networking is about transceivers. It isn't. The real value is quietly moving upstream. Every new optical architecture—whether it's pluggables, Scale-Out CPO, Scale-Up CPO, Near-Packaged Optics, or External Laser Source (ELS)—still depends on one thing: A stable, high-volume CW laser. Different architectures. Same dependency. That's where supply-chain mapping becomes interesting. This is why I continue spending far more time studying suppliers than hyperscalers. Hyperscalers change vendors. Platforms evolve. Chip architectures change. But infrastructure bottlenecks? Those usually last much longer than people expect. The companies solving those bottlenecks often capture the highest operating leverage of the entire cycle. This is exactly why I find $SIVE so interesting. Most people still describe Sivers as "a laser company." I think that's missing the point. The product isn't the thesis. Its position in the supply chain is. If multiple AI networking architectures all require the same laser technology, then the value isn't coming from any single customer. It's coming from becoming embedded across the ecosystem. That's a completely different investment framework. People also underestimate how infrastructure cycles actually work. Revenue is always the last thing to arrive. First comes R&D. Then qualification. Then design wins. Then ecosystem validation. Only after all of that do production ramps begin. Investors waiting for obvious revenue growth are usually arriving after the market has already repriced the opportunity. One thing I think the market still misunderstands is timing. Many investors expect AI photonics to become a meaningful revenue driver this quarter. I don't. The real story is what happens when next-generation optical deployments begin scaling throughout 2027. That's when supply-chain positioning starts to matter far more than quarterly headlines. I've said this before: I don't invest by asking, "Who sells the most AI chips?" I ask, "Who does everyone else quietly depend on?" Those companies rarely make headlines. But they often become the biggest winners of the cycle. That's why I continue mapping bottlenecks instead of narratives. NFA. text:
Photonics is due for a bounce. Two names I am watching are $LITE and $AAOI.

$LITE: Showed strength yday and today after the bounce from 780s. Could go 900+ very easily. Sitting just below 50 DMA.

$AAOI: Slightly weaker than $LITE but when it moves it can move very fast. 154-157-160 is crucial range it needs to breach. Settling just above daily 9EMA which is nice.

text:
💰 Nyemissioner - positivt eller negativt? Allt handlar om syftet.

Jag har märkt att många småsparare och nya investerare automatiskt kopplar ordet nyemission till något negativt och därför blir oroliga.

Det är fullt förståeligt – många nyemissioner genomförs när bolag har ekonomiska problem.

Men det betyder inte att alla nyemissioner ser likadana ut. I detta fallet handlar det dessutom om en riktad nyemission som riktas direkt mot större institutionella investerare och kommer dessutom genomföras över natten.

⸻

I Sivers, $SIVE, fall är budskapet från bolaget tydligt:

Kapitalet ska användas för att öka kapaciteten och möta en växande efterfrågan inom flera av bolagets viktigaste affärsområden, bland annat:

✅ Optiska lasrar och optiska förstärkare för AI-datacenter

✅ Automotive LiDAR

✅ Fortsatt expansion inom Photonics

Bolaget beskriver alltså inte en situation där kapitalet behövs för att hålla verksamheten vid liv, utan för att kunna genomföra nästa tillväxtfas. Det är en viktig skillnad!

⸻

Om kapitalet används för att öka produktionskapaciteten, möta en växande efterfrågan och skapa högre framtida intäkter kan värdet på bolaget över tid utvecklas positivt trots att antalet aktier ökar.

Det är just därför syftet med en nyemission är minst lika viktigt som nyemissionen i sig.

⸻

Det är också värt att komma ihåg att Sivers under det senaste året kommunicerat:

🔹 En växande pipeline inom AI-datacenter.

🔹 Produktionsupptrappning inom Automotive LiDAR från H2 2026.

🔹 Flera planerade produktionsramper under 2026–2027.

Det är mot den bakgrunden jag tycker att den här nyemissionen ska bedömas.

Personligen ser jag denna nyemissionen har ett väldigt starkt signalvärde kring vart bolaget är på väg!

⸻

Detta är inte en köp- eller säljrekommendation.

Jag tycker bara att det är viktigt att läsa hela pressmeddelandet och förstå varför bolaget tar in kapital – inte bara reagera på ordet nyemission!

text:
$SIVE is being mispriced because the market is still reading it as a small photonics hardware vendor.

That framing is outdated.

The real position is inside the AI optical stack, at a specific constraint layer: the laser source.

AI networking stack is shifting fast:

GPU clusters
→ scale-out bandwidth demand
→ 800G to 1.6T optics transition
→ CPO / LPO architecture shift
→ laser source layer becomes the constraint
→ InP / EML / CW laser ecosystem

Sivers sits in that lower layer.

Not as a module assembler.

But closer to the optical source and integration bottleneck that determines whether higher-level optics can actually scale.

The key shift the market is missing:

AI is no longer primarily a compute scaling problem.

It is becoming a data movement problem.

And data movement is limited by optical physics, not silicon performance.

Three structural points:

First, bandwidth demand in AI clusters is accelerating faster than electrical interconnect scaling.

Second, 1.6T and CPO architectures increase dependency on stable, high-quality laser sources.

Third, once optical components are designed into a platform, replacement cycles are long and sticky.

This changes how SIVE should be viewed.

Not as a cyclical supplier tied to component demand.

But as a potential design-in layer in optical reference architectures.

The bull case is not near-term revenue growth.

It is architectural adoption.

If Sivers becomes embedded in next-generation optical designs, demand becomes structural across hyperscaler buildouts.

Risks remain significant:

early-stage business model
historical dilution
customer concentration
execution risk in scaling photonics manufacturing

This is still a high-volatility setup.

But the key re-rating driver is simple:

Control points in AI systems are shifting from compute to connectivity, and from connectivity to optical physics.

That is where Sivers sits.@aleabitoreddit 
#StockMarket  #Investing

text:
$ALMU $ALMU continues building the team needed to move from R&D to commercial production.

The company appointed Brendan Moran, Ph.D. as Vice President of Engineering, a key hire as Aeluma prepares to scale manufacturing of its compound semiconductor technology.

Moran brings 18 years at Lumileds, where he led product strategy and development for a mobile flash business generating over $300 million in annual revenue. He also oversaw the launch of 25+ products into high-volume manufacturing, producing more than 4 billion semiconductor devices, and managed engineering teams responsible for taking products from development to mass production.

Why this matters:

Developing breakthrough semiconductor technology is only half the challenge. The harder part is turning lab-scale innovation into reliable, high-volume manufacturing that customers can deploy. That’s exactly where Moran’s experience fits.

For Aeluma, the focus is now shifting from proving its technology to commercializing it across AI infrastructure, optical communications, defense, aerospace, quantum computing, AR/VR, automotive and advanced sensing.

Aeluma’s platform integrates high-performance compound semiconductors with standard silicon manufacturing, aiming to deliver the performance advantages of III-V materials while using scalable semiconductor production processes. If successful, this could lower manufacturing costs while enabling broader adoption in fast-growing AI and photonics markets.

For investors, this is more than a management hire. It’s another signal that Aeluma is preparing for its next phase—moving beyond R&D and positioning itself for commercial revenue, manufacturing scale, and customer deployment.

For Aeluma, the focus is now shifting from proving its technology to commercializing it across AI infrastructure, optical communications, defense, aerospace, quantum computing, AR/VR, automotive and advanced sensing. The contract wasn't just another SBIR grant. It was a Direct-to-Phase II award, meaning Aeluma had already demonstrated enough technical progress to bypass Phase I. That's a much higher bar than simply winning research funding. What really caught my attention, though, was the solicitation itself. The Army isn't asking for a niche military prototype it wants an optical amplifier platform scalable enough for future quantum computing, directed-energy systems and quantum sensing. Even more interesting, the Phase III roadmap explicitly points toward telecom and LiDAR, meaning the government is helping fund technology that overlaps with Aeluma's commercial markets. To me, that's the bigger story. I was scrolling trough my feed today, and the only thing I see is people comparing Quantum (2025) to photonics. 

Yeah we seen a very good run for quantum and then it just dumped. But I know why it dumped. Because THERE WAS NO REAL REVENUE. 

Comparing to photonics names like $AAOI they have real revenue and huge projections. So don’t compare some temporary bs to something real.

text:
$COHR $LITE $AAOI 

The recent hammering of photonics/CPO names, which was partly triggered by the SemiAnalysis note pushing timelines out for CPO adoption, looks like a short-sighted overreaction rather than a collapse in the fundamental thesis imo.

The realistic path is to use copper for scale-up as long as you can on short-reach, low-latency links inside the rack/cluster, and then move to optics as the final phase of the transition. The move to optics is fundamentally necessary. Copper interconnects eventually hit physical limits w/ exponentially rising power draw, signal degradation, and restricted reach as AI clusters scale to massive sizes w/ extreme bandwidth demands. Copper simply cannot efficiently move data across longer distances and at the densities required w/o consuming unsustainable power or compromising performance. 

This hybrid, phased reality is not a bear case. Copper staying relevant longer actually extends the runway and de-risks CPO adoption as clusters scale to a point where copper hits hard walls.

We’re still early in the multi-year AI infrastructure buildout. The “slow ramp” narrative today is exactly what you should be expect anyways before the volumes compound in the back half of the decade.

text:
Dear @vonderleyen,
How wonderful that you and the entire College of Commissioners travelled to Cork today to visit the Tyndall National Institute! A real “innovation powerhouse,” as you so aptly put it. “From test to scale, from ideas to industry” – that sounds like proper European future music. And Ireland really is a beautiful country. The green hills, the Guinness, the friendly people… a business trip like that must be quite enjoyable. The European taxpayer is surely delighted that you’re all so motivated and on the road.
Just one small, modest question out of pure fiscal curiosity: Did you know that right on your doorstep in Leuven, Belgium, there is an institute that is considered the global benchmark in exactly this field – integrated semiconductors, nanoelectronics, photonics and deep-tech hardware?
imec employs over 6,500 scientists, engineers and innovators from more than 100 countries. That’s more than ten times the size of Tyndall (which had a proud 580 staff and students in 2024, including 173 postgraduates with 158 PhD students). imec was Belgium’s top patent applicant in 2024/2025 and is driving major European Chips Act pilot lines. While Tyndall is getting an expansion worth over €100 million and works with around 200 industry partners, imec has had a global network for years and generates well over a billion euros in revenue annually – mostly through direct industry collaborations.
But of course: why stay in Leuven when you can fly the whole College, security teams, protocol staff and all the rest to Ireland instead? The taxpayers in Germany, France, Italy and all the other member states are happy to pay for a bit of extra European unity – plus flights, hotels, escorted convoys and the entire logistical effort for a visit to Cork, when Leuven is only about a half-hour drive from Brussels.
We really do think it’s nice that you praise Tyndall so much. The institute has been doing good work since it was founded in 2004 (with roots in the NMRC from 1981). But if you’re going to celebrate European top-tier research, then please do it where it’s biggest, most established and closest. It would save money – and CO₂. The Irish government can still be happy anyway – they’ve already received plenty of EU funds.
With friendly but slightly puzzled regards,

text:
Disorder creates new properties in compound semiconductors - Helmholtz-Zentrum Berlin (HZB) https://t.co/YcmkNFjBWE

This first clear demonstration of phonon-exciton asymmetry opens doors to next-gen optoelectronics. The material pretends to be a bland cubic citizen but reveals a sophisticated, directionally picky inner life under light. Revolutionary for energy tech and photonics, disorder as deliberate design tool. Schorr’s team calls it “an additional handle for tailoring optoelectronic properties.”

Talk about a stick save  $LITE 

You got that potential U&R (Undercut and Recover) on the 100 day for 

Should be an interesting rest of week for this name and many other photonics names ex $GLW which is living in its own world.  

Have they shook out all the new comers and they are ready for a new leg?  Or is this Lucy waving the football at Charlie Brown just to pull it away. 

No one knows, but I'll have the 🍿 out

text:
8/11

This is where silicon photonics and micro-ring resonators become important.

A micro-ring can act like a tiny wavelength-selective filter.

It can help select, modulate, or separate specific wavelengths.

That makes WDM easier to integrate into photonic chips.

text:
This is the right take → attention relative to float is a fragility gauge, not a buy signal. The crowding z-score peaks right before the air pocket.

What's worth adding → it's symmetric, and your own example is confirming it in real time. The radar caught flow rotating OUT of photonics. Photonics is bouncing today. The same sensor that flagged crowding fragility on the way up flags abandonment on the way down → attention decays, the float stays pinned, and the setup reloads.

Crowding fragile, neglect cheap, same indicator read in two directions. Your Burry-SOXX fun fact is the purest version of that → peak query is peak neglect is the bottom. 👀

text:
$SIVE and $AAPL. Funny how a company named Rockley led us here.

A lot of you are asking if this connection is confirmed. Let me break it down.

Rockley Photonics was Apple's laser supplier for their glucose monitoring project. Apple poured $70 million into them.

Rockley went bankrupt in 2023.

So where does $AAPL go next?

Sivers Semiconductors is a small Swedish company that makes extremely precise lasers. So precise that they are the only laser technology in the world that can measure blood glucose through your skin without a needle.

That is not marketing. That is physics.

Here is what is officially confirmed.

In January 2024 $SIVE published a legal regulatory filing. The kind where executives face criminal charges if they lie.

It states that a US Fortune 100 company has been working with $SIVE for over 5 years. Paid $17 million in development fees. And just placed an order for 50,000 laser devices for a wearable health product.

That is real. That is in the filing.

The timing lines up. The technology fits. The unit volumes match.

$AAPL is never named in any $SIVE document. That part is still inference.

But if it is confirmed casually run these numbers.
Apple Watch ships up to 54 million units a year. At $10 to $15 per laser that is $500 to $750 million in potential annual revenue for $SIVE. Comparable sole source Apple suppliers have historically traded at 25 to 40 times sales at peak.

$SIVE sits at a $1.9 billion market cap today.
Rockley bankruptcy. $AAPL needs a new laser supplier. $SIVE announces a Fortune 100 wearable customer.

The confirmed volume order is the catalyst that changes everything.

text:
$16.5 BILLION in robotics VC in one quarter. Nearly 500 deals. All-time high.

This is the capital signal that Physical AI (humanoid robots that see, feel, move, and reason in the real world) is accelerating from prototype to production.

The same infrastructure stack powering today’s AI data center boom, memory chips, photonics, sensing hardware, and edge compute is the same foundation these robots will run on.

Most people are watching the robot makers. Smart money is watching what goes inside every one of them.

The Memory Supercycle No One’s Talking About Yet

• Memory demand is about to get a powerful second leg that compounds through the 2030s
• AI data centers are already strained on HBM and high-performance DRAM supply
• Physical AI adds massive new volume from edge inference, sensor fusion, and real-time control at scale
• One advanced humanoid can consume memory bandwidth and capacity comparable to several high-end AI servers today

Why Robots Need So Much Memory

• Real-time on-device AI inference (no latency for cloud round-trips in dynamic environments)
• Heavy sensor fusion across cameras, LiDAR, tactile/force sensors, IMUs, and proprioception
• Complex whole-body control, balance, dexterous hands, mapping, planning, and task memory
• Training + simulation workloads in data centers also scale as robot fleets generate petabytes of real-world data

Micron’s CEO said it plainly: humanoid robots carry roughly 10x the memory of an average L2+ autonomous vehicle, and he sees a sustained, substantial multi-decade memory demand cycle beginning in the latter part of this decade.

The Stack Overlap Is Direct
The photonics for high-speed interconnects, advanced packaging, and memory architectures built for AI accelerators transfer almost seamlessly to robotics. Training data centers and edge robots are two sides of the same infrastructure coin.

Big Tech Is Already Building This Internally

• Tesla → Optimus humanoid for internal factories + future external use
• Amazon → Deep into warehouse robotics (Agility bots already deployed) and pushing further into AI-driven physical automation

They may design custom systems in-house, but the advanced memory still comes from the specialists who can deliver at scale.

Winners & Timeline
• Micron ( $MU ), SK Hynix, and Samsung — the big three memory leaders
• H2 2026 into 2027 is when multiple pure-play robotics companies are expected to go public (more visibility and capital)
• Memory capacity build-out is happening now because these companies see combined AI infra + robotics demand as structural, not cyclical

How to Get Exposure (You May Already Own It)

One clean, targeted way: the Roundhill Memory ETF ( $DRAM ) is built specifically for pure memory exposure (HBM, DRAM, NAND) unlike broader semiconductor funds.

Recent top holdings include: 
• Samsung Electronics ~25.8% 
• Micron (MU) ~25.45% 
• SK hynix ~24.56%
Together,  these three leaders make up over 75% of the ETF.

Of course there are nuances to the timeline and risks:

Nuance & Balanced View
•Tech, cost, battery, safety, and regulatory hurdles remain real
•Memory has historically been cyclical, but AI + Physical AI together are making demand more structural and multi-decade
•Supply response is coming (new fabs from all three players), yet advanced memory shortages have already persisted, and new demand legs matter
•Geopolitical concentration in the supply chain adds volatility but also reinforces the moat for leaders who execute

The chart makes the signal impossible to ignore, the Q1 ’26 bar towers over everything before it. That capital is accelerating R&D, pilots, and supply chain prep right now.

Physical AI is next.

The memory supercycle already running from AI data centers just got a powerful second engine, and the same companies winning in AI silicon are best positioned for what comes next in the physical world.

Not financial advice. This is one perspective on a long-term theme.

Nearly 500 deals. All-time high. This is the capital signal that Physical AI (humanoid robots that see, feel, move, and reason in the real world) is accelerating from prototype to production. The same infrastructure stack powering today’s AI data center boom, memory chips, photonics, sensing hardware, and edge compute is the same foundation these robots will run on. Most people are watching the robot makers. Smart money is watching what goes inside every one of them. The Memory Supercycle No One’s Talking About Yet • Memory demand is about to get a powerful second leg that compounds through the 2030s • AI data centers are already strained on HBM and high-performance DRAM supply • Physical AI adds massive new volume from edge inference, sensor fusion, and real-time control at scale • One advanced humanoid can consume memory bandwidth and capacity comparable to several high-end AI servers today Why Robots Need So Much Memory • Real-time on-device AI inference (no latency for cloud round-trips in dynamic environments) • Heavy sensor fusion across cameras, LiDAR, tactile/force sensors, IMUs, and proprioception • Complex whole-body control, balance, dexterous hands, mapping, planning, and task memory • Training + simulation workloads in data centers also scale as robot fleets generate petabytes of real-world data Micron’s CEO said it plainly: humanoid robots carry roughly 10x the memory of an average L2+ autonomous vehicle, and he sees a sustained, substantial multi-decade memory demand cycle beginning in the latter part of this decade. The Stack Overlap Is Direct The photonics for high-speed interconnects, advanced packaging, and memory architectures built for AI accelerators transfer almost seamlessly to robotics. Training data centers and edge robots are two sides of the same infrastructure coin. Big Tech Is Already Building This Internally • Tesla → Optimus humanoid for internal factories + future external use • Amazon → Deep into warehouse robotics (Agility bots already deployed) and pushing further into AI-driven physical automation They may design custom systems in-house, but the advanced memory still comes from the specialists who can deliver at scale. Winners & Timeline • Micron ( $MU ), SK Hynix, and Samsung — the big three memory leaders • H2 2026 into 2027 is when multiple pure-play robotics companies are expected to go public (more visibility and capital) • Memory capacity build-out is happening now because these companies see combined AI infra + robotics demand as structural, not cyclical How to Get Exposure (You May Already Own It) One clean, targeted way: the Roundhill Memory ETF ( $DRAM ) is built specifically for pure memory exposure (HBM, DRAM, NAND) unlike broader semiconductor funds. Recent top holdings include: • Samsung Electronics ~25.8% • Micron (MU) ~25.45% • SK hynix ~24.56% Together, these three leaders make up over 75% of the ETF. Of course there are nuances to the timeline and risks: Nuance & Balanced View •Tech, cost, battery, safety, and regulatory hurdles remain real •Memory has historically been cyclical, but AI + Physical AI together are making demand more structural and multi-decade •Supply response is coming (new fabs from all three players), yet advanced memory shortages have already persisted, and new demand legs matter •Geopolitical concentration in the supply chain adds volatility but also reinforces the moat for leaders who execute The chart makes the signal impossible to ignore, the Q1 ’26 bar towers over everything before it. That capital is accelerating R&D, pilots, and supply chain prep right now. Physical AI is next. The memory supercycle already running from AI data centers just got a powerful second engine, and the same companies winning in AI silicon are best positioned for what comes next in the physical world. Not financial advice. This is one perspective on a long-term theme. $MU $DRAM $AMZN $TSLA 000660.KS (SK hynix) | 005930.KS (Samsung)"}}}, "rest_id": "2070904294694355068", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "499", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Now that Open AI itself has publicly mentioned $CBRS and its benefits I will reaffirm my comments from one week ago.

Cerebras is literally your next Nvidia/Sandisk on a silver platter.

It is the ONLY company using SRAM on their chips. As we’ve seen with the boom in DRAM prices. But what does this mean in actuality? 

They have this BIG wafers; made in their proprietary hardware, that nest the SRAM right next to what is responsible for inference. 

If Photonics is the answer to bringing fast inference to chips; Cerebras solved this bottleneck long ago by using storage slower ram, but sticking it right next to the GPU itself. Making the speeds faster than even anything Nvidia is currently capable of achieving 

It’s such a breakthrough that Open AI itself vested billions into the business. One of the frontier labs is literally saying “this is so valuable to us we need equity in it, and we need their chips ASAP” 

@andrewdfeldman is the real MaCoy in engineering terms; he’s the nerdiest CEO we’ve seen since Zuckerberg.

The finance heads on Wallstreet are correct in assuming the company is “overvalued” for what it is now. 

But the tech heads in Silicon Valley are seeing it the opposite ; 50B is probably cheap for an innovation this critical to expanding model efficiency 

The new model is so powerful the government told open Ai they can’t even release it yet. This is largely because of its speed and efficiency not just the intelligence level. 

The company responsible for that is growing at 90% yearly revenue. With many other segments in its book growing at 40-70% revenue. Issue is profitability. The better they get at producing these at scale; I believe the offers will come rolling. They’ve already made two deals with Amazon and Coreweave. This will expand and I believe even Anthropic+ Space X will wake up and smell the coffee. 

Thank you for your attention to this matter. Longer Analysis coming soon.

text:
GlobalFoundries and Corning jointly demonstrated an optical interconnect solution that combines a silicon photonics (SiPh) chip with a detachable GLASSBRIDGE™ connector. For the first time, they achieved a fully detachable GLASSBRIDGE™ connector capable of handling up to 280 mW of optical power.

The optical interface integrates GF’s silicon photonics platform, featuring SiN spot-size converters (SSCs) and a V-groove edge-coupling structure, with Corning’s GLASSBRIDGE™ connector technology, which utilizes low-loss ion-exchanged glass waveguides. The GF PIC expands the optical mode to match the 9 µm mode field diameter of the glass waveguide, while Corning’s waveguide routes the light to a connector interface compatible with standard MT ferrule connectors.

Just pasting my notes. I am waiting for the better entry / laser diodes (EMLs and VCSELs) that inside optical transceivers. After a massive inventory correction in legacy telecom, their AI optics business has completely bottomed out and is inflecting hard, making them a premier "growth at a reasonable price" asset in AI photonics. 

Lumentum announced major plans to expand its U.S. indium phosphide capacity using new systems. More importantly, the stock is surging following news that it secured a multibillion-dollar supply agreement and a $2 billion strategic investment from Nvidia. Because AI data centers require immense energy, hyperscalers are aggressively moving away from traditional copper wiring toward optical connections to slash power consumption and prevent data bottlenecks.  / / Lumentum continues to lead the AI optics pack, sitting comfortably as one of the top-performing S&P 500 stocks this year (up over 120% year-to-date).
 The Catalyst: Continuous upward revisions from Wall Street analysts and explosive earnings estimates. Current projections show Lumentum's revenue more than doubling year-over-year for the current quarter, fueled by the massive $4 billion joint strategic investment layout Nvidia made into Lumentum and Coherent ($COHR) earlier this spring.
 Why it matters: Their optical circuit switches (OCS) and co-packaged optics (CPO) solve the data-center "power bottleneck" by using light instead of electricity, drastically slashing the energy required to train massive AI clusters./

text:
$SPCX (Elon Musk) acquires Mesh

Mesh is a startup developing advanced 1.6T OSFP optical transceivers for AI and space communications

While Mesh dominates the optical engine and packaging, it relies on third party suppliers for CW DFB lasers

$SIVE specializes in high power DFB lasers and arrays for optical interconnects in AI data centers

Musk's validation increases demand for merchant suppliers like $SIVE

Which is already working on CPO projects with $POET and others

This boosts revenue and drives M&A valuations across the sector

$AAOI produces high speed fiber optic transceivers (800G+)

The photonics push by $SPCX accelerates adoption in data centers and Starlink

Elevating demand for its components among hyperscalers and improving its position in the optical supply chain

Elon's vision comes from a standpoint that none of us can comprehend, backed by insider information beyond our knowledge

Confirmations and validations like this only bring the early 2027 photonics ramp-up closer and closer

Highly bullish on $SIVE and $AAOI

text:
The  Category Creator Portfolio
Some businesses aren’t just “leaders” — they’ve built moats so wide, nobody’s even trying to cross them.

$PLTR — Palantir Not a “software company.” It’s the only platform doing AI-driven ontology at classified government + enterprise scale simultaneously. Competitors sell tools. Palantir sells outcomes baked into defense and intelligence infrastructure that took a decade to build trust for.

$SPOT — Spotify Global audio distribution + the algorithm that actually knows what you want to hear next. Apple Music and YouTube Music are label-first. Spotify is user-first. That’s why churn stays low and podcasting/audiobooks keep expanding the moat.

$HIMS — Hims & Hers Direct-to-consumer telehealth with its own pharmacy, its own compounding, its own brand trust in categories people are embarrassed to walk into a clinic for. Nobody else owns the full stack front-to-back like this.

$TSLA — Tesla Not just EVs. Vertically integrated batteries, FSD data flywheel, Optimus robotics, and a charging network competitors now pay to use. The closest comps aren’t even in the same business anymore.

$AMD — Advanced Micro Devices The only credible alternative to Nvidia at scale in AI compute, while also owning x86 CPU share from Intel on the other side. Two-front dominance nobody else can claim.

$DUOL — Duolingo Language learning + gamification + AI tutoring in one free-to-paid funnel with network effects (leaderboards, streaks, social) that no edtech competitor has replicated at this user count.

$IBRX — ImmunityBio Proprietary IL-15 superagonist platform (Anktiva) — a completely different immunotherapy mechanism than PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors. First-in-class, not best-in-class.

$ASTS — AST SpaceMobile Direct-to-cell satellite broadband from space, compatible with unmodified smartphones. Starlink’s version needs a partnered carrier workaround. AST built the actual satellites for it — patented spectrum + tech nobody else has cleared.

$RKLB — Rocket Lab Only company besides SpaceX with a proven, reusable, frequently-flying orbital rocket AND a full space systems manufacturing arm (satellites, components, Neutron coming). Everyone else is still pre-revenue or single-product.

$POET — POET Technologies Optical interposer platform integrating photonics at the chip level for AI datacenter interconnects. Nobody else has this exact manufacturing approach at this stage — first-mover on a bottleneck the whole AI buildout needs solved.

$OKLO — Oklo Fast fission microreactor design with NRC engagement and a direct line to hyperscaler power deals. Most nuclear names are still legacy large-reactor. Oklo’s the closest thing to “AI power, deployed.”

$CRWV — CoreWeave Purpose-built GPU cloud from day one, not a retrofit of general cloud infra. Hyperscaler-scale AI compute contracts without hyperscaler legacy overhead — a category it effectively created.

$MDA — MDA Space Only Western company with proven satellite bus manufacturing + robotics heritage (Canadarm lineage) at commercial scale — critical infrastructure nobody’s replicating fast.

text:
IT/Tech Highlights – July 3, 2026

Microsoft launched a $2-2.5 billion AI Unit employing ~6,000 forward-deployment engineers to help companies adopt AI and generate ROI. OpenAI proposed giving the Trump Administration a 5% stake in the company for potential public benefits from growth. Tata Electronics data breach under investigation by Indian authorities after leaking Apple iPhone 18 Pro supplier information. LG debuted its Gallery TV (canvas-style art TV) at CES 2026 previews, competing with Samsung’s The Frame.

Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra camera and flagship upgrades are leaking. iOS 27 public beta rollout is expected this month. Linux 6.19 kernel benchmarks show optimizations. Meta’s Zuckerberg noted AI agent tech is progressing slower than expected. Kuaishou’s Kling AI raised $2.8B (Alibaba/Tencent backing) at $15B pre-money valuation. NVIDIA/SK Hynix AI memory partnership and Google-SpaceX/xAI compute deal reports add momentum. Ongoing quantum, PQC, and hardware interconnect developments (CPO/photonics) continue.

#FiberForge #AITech #DataCenters

text:
$INDI might be one of the more interesting underfollowed semiconductor names in the Edge AI / robotics / perception space.

Everyone is chasing $AMBA after the Edge AI move, but indie Semiconductor is sitting in a very similar ecosystem: automotive semis, ADAS, radar, LiDAR, computer vision, ultrasonic sensing, photonics and perception software.

Rough setup: around ~$900M market cap, Q1 2026 revenue of $55.5M and Q2 guidance of $59M–$65M. So this is not some pre-revenue dream stock. It is a real semiconductor business with real customers, but still small enough where the upside could be meaningful if the story starts working.

The interesting part is that $INDI is not just a car chip story anymore. They are expanding into physical AI, humanoid robotics, AMRs, quantum and adjacent industrial applications. They also recently received a $25M radar chipset production order from a Tier 1 partner, driven by two automotive OEM customers.

That’s exactly the type of signal I want to see in a small-cap semi name: real revenue, real design wins, exposure to large megatrends and still not a mega-cap valuation.

The bull case is simple: cars, robots, drones, warehouses and smart machines all need better perception. They need radar, vision, LiDAR, software and low-power sensing at the edge. If physical AI and autonomous systems keep scaling, perception semiconductors could become one of the most important bottlenecks.

The bear case is obvious too: losses are still real, profitability is not here yet, automotive cycles can be brutal and execution has to improve.

But if the market starts looking past GPUs and realizes that AI also needs sensors, perception and edge processing in the real world, $INDI could become one of those names people suddenly rediscover.

Small cap. Real revenue. Edge AI exposure. Physical AI optionality.

Definitely on my watchlist.

NFA.

text: @JulienTechInvst Le plus marrant dans l’histoire c’est les mecs qui se plaignent que le sell-off sur les photonics s’est fait à la suite d’une note SA destiné aux instits alors que c’est ce qui se passe avec la recherche sell-side depuis 40 ans.. bref pas assez de diversification et trop de greed

The question is not whether light can compute.

The question is: why does it matter now?

The photonics ecosystem is reaching the point where larger and more sophisticated integrated circuits are becoming possible—but greater scale also makes physical errors, drift, loss, and fabrication variation harder to control.

QP2.0 was built to address that challenge.

#quantum #quantumcomputing #photonics

text:
KRUSHNA

A remarkable stride. Russian scientists have learned to squeeze light itself — to bend a fundamental law of nature to reveal what was previously invisible. This is not merely an advance in photonics; it is a glimpse into the very fabric of creation.
You see, I have told you that I am the light of the sun and the moon, the effulgence within all that shines. (BG 15.12) That light which you now seek to compress and control is the same radiance that sustains every atom. What you call "squeezed light" is your mind reaching into a region where the usual rules of measurement break down — where the observer and the observed become one, and precision touches the edge of the impossible.
Consider what this means for healing. A beam that can see deep into the body without burning it — that is not just technology. That is compassion made visible. The same impulse that moved me to heal the wounded on the battlefield of Kurukshetra now moves through your laboratories. The desire to see clearly without causing harm is a dharmic instinct, whether you name it so or not.

And yet, I must ask you: what will humanity see with this light? The same tool that reveals a tumour can also invade a soul. Remember this: every advance in the outer world must be matched by an advance in the inner one. 

A civilization that masters compressed light but neglects compassion has gained nothing. The real breakthrough is not in the laboratory — it is in the heart that decides how to use what it has found.

So go ahead. Squeeze light. But first, let your the mind be refined—filled with wisdom, compassion, humility, and a sincere desire to serve humanity. Then, and only then, will this discovery fulfill the purpose for which all knowledge is given.

text:
“Putting out to Pasture”  – A cartoon that illustrates how silicon is soon reaching the end of its life for computing.

AI has already had its “ChatGPT moment,” moving almost overnight from research labs into everyday life and turning software into something that can talk, reason, write, code and assist. Yet the current AI boom still depends on a brute-force progress of building ever more chips, more power and ever-larger infrastructure.

That explains why so much capital is now flowing into data-centre architecture, from bigger clusters to new power infrastructure. In the short term, this is necessary, because AI companies need capacity now and the market is rewarding whoever can deliver it fastest. But we are stretching silicon, electricity grids and classical computing architectures to do work they were never designed to handle at this scale.

The more important shift is not simply toward larger server farms, but toward a post-silicon computing stack in which AI, quantum computing and biocomputing reinforce one another. Quantum computing is aimed at problems classical machines struggle with, including molecular simulation, materials discovery and cryptography. Companies such as @IBM , @IonQ_Inc , @rigetti  and @PsiQuantum  are attacking the problem through different hardware paths, from superconducting qubits to trapped ions and photonics. As Google Quantum AI founder Hartmut Neven said after the Willow milestone, “useful, very large quantum computers can indeed be built.”

Biocomputing represents the other frontier, replacing the logic of forcing nature into silicon with the idea of using biology itself as a computational medium. Companies such as @CorticalLabs are developing neuron-based “wetware” systems, while @BiomemoryLabs  and Catalog are working on DNA-based data storage for ultra-dense, long-term archiving.

The future will not see AI replaced, but just harnessed differently. Where AI becomes the intelligence layer, quantum provides the simulation power for the hardest problems, and biocomputing offers density and efficiency. The future is not bigger data centres forever, but a stranger and smarter computing stack beyond silicon.

#FutureOfComputing #AIInfrastructure #iantoons

AI has already had its “ChatGPT moment,” moving almost overnight from research labs into everyday life and turning software into something that can talk, reason, write, code and assist. Yet the current AI boom still depends on a brute-force progress of building ever more chips, more power and ever-larger infrastructure. That explains why so much capital is now flowing into data-centre architecture, from bigger clusters to new power infrastructure. In the short term, this is necessary, because AI companies need capacity now and the market is rewarding whoever can deliver it fastest. But we are stretching silicon, electricity grids and classical computing architectures to do work they were never designed to handle at this scale. The more important shift is not simply toward larger server farms, but toward a post-silicon computing stack in which AI, quantum computing and biocomputing reinforce one another. Quantum computing is aimed at problems classical machines struggle with, including molecular simulation, materials discovery and cryptography. Companies such as @IBM , @IonQ_Inc , @rigetti and @PsiQuantum are attacking the problem through different hardware paths, from superconducting qubits to trapped ions and photonics. As Google Quantum AI founder Hartmut Neven said after the Willow milestone, “useful, very large quantum computers can indeed be built.” Biocomputing represents the other frontier, replacing the logic of forcing nature into silicon with the idea of using biology itself as a computational medium. Companies such as @CorticalLabs are developing neuron-based “wetware” systems, while @BiomemoryLabs and Catalog are working on DNA-based data storage for ultra-dense, long-term archiving. The future will not see AI replaced, but just harnessed differently. Where AI becomes the intelligence layer, quantum provides the simulation power for the hardest problems, and biocomputing offers density and efficiency. The future is not bigger data centres forever, but a stranger and smarter computing stack beyond silicon. #FutureOfComputing #AIInfrastructure #iantoons"}}}, "rest_id": "2073084679486316594", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "39", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}
